Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Dec 3, 2021 at 9:27 AM
Reactions : Like, Wow, Love — Milo Talon, DaddyfromNaboo, 12Rogues
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Hey all,

I'm plunging into the 5ft Falcon. As I  have gathered  my info I have noticed that a lot of the reference has been lost due to bad links, missing members, etc. My goal here is to not only show my progress, but to collect as much info as I can hosted via the RPF so hopefully the info will always be here. I've already purchased Geek1138's awesome 3d printing files, but am still interested in the actual models.

I've taken the most comprehensive list I could find and started to price/find the kits. My goal is to create a document with pics of each model that can be shared for future reference. So far I've just started with the Airfix kits. What I could really use help with is knowing how many of each kit is needed. If you know and could toss that info my way I'll add it to the document.

Scifikitbash.com (My kit scan site)

SS Falcon Kit List

SS Falcon Part Maps psds

SS Falcon Part Maps jpgs

SS Falcon MY PARTS MAPS

FIles for 3D Printing

Bandai Scans

Post #12  (forum post #5256912)
Author    : Pyramidrep
Date      : Dec 7, 2021 at 9:07 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Tsophika said:

			Please don't pay $500 for a Harrier.

Sold listing search will be very helpful and most models can be had fairly, with patience.

Craig
		

Click to expand...

I nearly fainted when I saw the $500 price on the Airfix Harrier. Here in Europe the price range is anywhere between €70 to €120. If you’re patient, you might be able to find and buy one at a bargain.

The large scale 1/12 Tamiya kits are also expensive ( € 150 to €250) and again , you can find the odd kit cheaper at auction on eBay.

The 1/15 scale Bandai kits (Hummel and Sturmgeschutz iv ) are “pigs” to find and buy.(  €150- € 250)

I had a look through the list & found a number that were not used on this miniature. These being:

Airfix 1/24 Stuka

Airfix 1/76 Scorpion

AMT 1/25 Transit Dump Truck

Hasegawa 1/72 Tiger 1

Italeri 1/35 Kubelwagen

Nichimo 1/35 Jagdpanther

Tamiya 1/12 Ferrari 312T

Tamiya 1/12 Tyrrell P34 6 Wheeler

Tamiya 1/12 Matra

Tamiya 1/35 8-Ton Semi Track 20mm Flakvierling

Tamiya 1/35 M16/M21 Halftracks

Tamiya 1/35 M48

The Tamiya 1/35 M4 Sherman you have listed is the wrong type/variant, you need the M4 as per picture here. 
		
		
	

	

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/20211210_102945-jpg.1519689/]

Hello. Just joined.

If you were into resin 3D printing, I could really help you out. My own project is to build a 3D printed, 32" inch studio scale MF. But the detail is based on the 5 footer. So, I have been collecting digital versions of most of the ubiquitous and defining kit parts, like the Wankel Rotary engine. The 8Rad. Sealab III and a whole bunch of others.

Does the printed mandible structure come out at exactly 2" thick?

I only recently noticed that the armour plating only partially covers the pit holes, yet a lip is visible around the entire diameter.

Meaning that much of the overhang starts with the original acrylic used to form the mandibles themselves.

It doesn't appear to be very thick though. Perhaps 1/8" and certainly no more than 3/16". The picture below is of the upper P/S aft pit and it is only half covered with styrene armour plating.

I understand that you are limited to some degree with the files that you are using to create the model when 3D printing. Unless you make massive alterations along the way.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/screen-shot-2021-12-17-at-8-48-38-am-png.1521413/]

Post #58  (forum post #5264127)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Dec 31, 2021 at 3:30 AM
Reactions : Like, Wow — Vacformedhero, Dimitri-Ajith, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

One thing I learned on my Falcon building journey is to always strive to obtain the vintage or original issues of the donor kits!. There are many kits that have been reissued over the years, but more than likely have been remolded or altered. By buying the vintage or original issues you know you have the exact same kit ILM used, with no surprise of missing parts, altered parts or a completely re-tooled kit!.

I should know...been there...done that!. Quite a few times i have been caught out thinking a newer issue would suffice...nope!. 

Tamiya is a good example of a manufacturer altering molds over the years. The box art might look the same, but the contents not so much!.

Just be aware...check sprue scans...ask around...just don't jump on a kit like say the Tamiya 1/35 Tiger 1 newer issue & expect to find the same parts as ILM used back in 1976!. 

Stu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The chassis used on the inner mandible walls should be hopefully in most if not all kits? not sure though...

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/screen-shot-2021-11-01-at-12-52-13-pm-png.1529154/]

One day we'll have to get all the 5 footers together, like someone mentioned. As slow as I am, I just want to get something done before I croak, LOL. Cheers,

Joe

Post #113  (forum post #5271735)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Jan 16, 2022 at 6:00 PM
Reactions : Like — Vacformedhero, Bjorn, Studio Kitbash
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Hey chaps. A word of warning regarding the recent & ongoing reissues of the AMT & Ertl 1/25 trucks.

I bought the Peterbilt Wrecker for another project & the Ladder Chief just to see what the parts were like inside & it seems to be a mixed bag!.

The Ladder Chief looks good, parts look good for the most part, all Falcon donor parts there & unaltered as far as i can tell.

But the Wrecker parts seem to be altered or very soft on detail, not crisp like the original issues & decal sheet different too. Sometimes you also need the decals from these kits.

So i am very dubious now of these new reissues, not being straight reissues of their vintage predecessors!.

Another reminder to stick to my rule of only buy vintage issue kits, costly i know, but peace of mind knowing you will have crisp & original donor parts!.

Oh also...the newer Airfix repops of their older issues are very poor too, such as the 0/400, parts details very soft & need much cleaning up!.

Vintage...this is the way!
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #125  (forum post #5272768)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Jan 19, 2022 at 7:17 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, swgeek, Dimitri-Ajith
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

At the very least, you'll need the California Hauler for one of the lower un-ID'ed sections on MF027:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1642605161858-png.1535743/]

The L/R railings, in blue on the picture, are actually a composite of two other pieces.  The chassis rails are from the Kenworth W-925, and are what's “leftover” after making the cut for the upper mandible railings.  On TOP of this chassis rail, at the end (by the Mauretania funnels), sits chrome part #547 from the California Hauler:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1642605369838-png.1535744/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #147  (forum post #5277111)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The merge collector in the Tamiya 49B isn't shaped like that. There was another Lotus released by Imai in the '60s, but also incorrect.

Bjorn did jog my memory though. The 312B part could be sliced and puttied to resemble the tips but it's hard to say definitively. Those are Dave Jones' hands holding the completed gun turret but the construction details were lost to time.

Craig

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Nice work on the rear map John!

I remember seeing a callout to the tips being from the Lotus 49B in Nicks the original ID thread here. (it's an affliction and I need help)...

But similar to Craig, I can't find a piece that looks like this it in the period kit. 

David3, I just did a quick search of that 49B picture on the world wide web and alas, it seems to be from a very heavily modified, almost scratch built model Lotus. Perhaps the new 2014/2015 re-release of this kit has something resembling this?

At this stage I'm siding with Craig that it was probably hand modified much like the Me109/Kettenkrad struts for engine petals and Y-Wing engine nacelles. Putty or 3D printing is likely the best option. But not giving up the search altogether.

It seems to me that master model makers Sean Sides and Stuart Brown used 3D printing for their build going off pics in their Super Build Threads or SBT's.

Post #154  (forum post #5277541)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Jan 28, 2022 at 9:50 AM
Reactions : Love, Like — Bjorn, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The AMT Ladder Chief has the exact same engine parts, #123/124, as the California Hauler (Part #547), for dressing up the ends of the cut-offs from the Kenworth W-925 chassis rails on the center underside.  The Ladder Chief parts are NOT chromed, so I suspect these would be easier to use and would take paint better.

This is in reference to the un-ID'ed lower section on MF027, in reference to my previous Post #125 on this thread.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #155  (forum post #5277578)
Author    : Tsophika
Date      : Jan 28, 2022 at 11:33 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Bjorn, Dimitri-Ajith
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Tamiya 49/49B are the same kit. The B has an added sprue.

Tamiya did put out slight retools of their F-1 kits that affect Millennium Falcon parts. I've found both versions imported by MRC in the 70s.

[IMAGE: 1181960-5ee568049ca7c66866756c259efba614.jpg]

It also happened with the Honda.

[IMAGE: 1181964-f0249e642676976a0a68a9ce7d78c356.jpg]

There was something with the M23 too but that escapes me now.

Craig

This  is the right kit for the elevator parts that attach to the Messer struts right?

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/v_us_qknq60ejqrzlr9xpokxpjbtotiero0cdcroj8xa1m7wuq-png.1539589/]

Post #157  (forum post #5277825)
Author    : Bjorn
Date      : Jan 28, 2022 at 9:11 PM
Reactions : Like — Searun, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Yes, this was the 1st release but it also seems that the tooling has changed little if at all over the years. 

Not sure about the latest Italeri release, but it seems that you can also use the Hasegawa, DML, Polistil and Revell.

Keep in mind that some of the engine parts used were mostly castings of this kit as they are frosted with other parts like the Harrow Loco as used in the top and bottom of the Jaw Box, and on the Y-wing.

Post #183  (forum post #5281727)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Feb 4, 2022 at 5:08 PM
Reactions : Like — Sparky70, joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Part #3 is actually Tamiya 1/25 Centurion. The 1/25 Chieftain is not a Falcon donor kit.

Only the vintage & early issues of the Centurion have the 'screwdriver' parts...later issues had this omitted from the sprues.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Create a new file in Photoshop and save it. Can just be a blank file, doesn't matter. Look at the destination that Photoshop wants to save it to, then look there for your missing file.

Photoshop does this to me daily - saves my files in places I wasn't expecting, then I have to go searching for them.

Post #264  (forum post #5291837)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Feb 26, 2022 at 4:49 AM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Are these plans still considered the most accurate, and have they ever been revised?

			https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/anh_mf_top_003-jpg.439126/
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #265  (forum post #5291970)
Author    : Bjorn
Date      : Feb 26, 2022 at 2:27 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Searun, David3
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

These amazing plans were created by a group led by vfxsup64 and maruska and from what I have seen, currently represent the most accurate drawings of the ANH "5 footer" to be published. eagle1 and t2sides had a big part in this collaboration.

I think the most recognisable issue with these drawings at this point is the cockpit diameter of 5.75". They have since established 6" as the correct diameter.

As I understand, after literally thousands of hours later, they are still being tweaked to be as accurate as possible. However between other projects and professional work/family obligations, any of this revised work is yet to be released.

If you haven't already done so, check out their build threads and Maruska's Falconer blog for a run down of their approach and findings.

I consider them must reads for would-be 5 foot builders.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #266  (forum post #5291972)
Author    : Dimitri-Ajith
Date      : Feb 26, 2022 at 2:35 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Bjorn, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

You can find the latest released version on Grabcad, not sure if they are the same as the drawing you posted. (I think the link is also on the Maruska Falconer page Bjorn mentioned)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #267  (forum post #5292044)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Feb 26, 2022 at 10:07 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Dimitri-Ajith
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Also, Maruska and Co's upper gun turret base OD is off by a smidge -- it's actually slightly larger than that, as both the photogrammetry method and the Bandai PG Falcon conversion method will show.  N.B.:  these two methods do not deliver the same answer, but a range of answers within 0.15" of each other, so you have to make an interpretation, guess, and commit to "your answer" at some point either way, no matter how you get there. Another way to figure it is to think about how ILM worked, and in what material.  So for the gun turret bases, the evidence suggests that the ILM guys started with a 12" round wooden disc, put it on a lathe, and started shaving away until they found the angle they liked.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Hey Larsen, 

That's a great question and one that I too have also been mulling over recently.

I could be wrong here, but I think the Version 1 drawing released on GrabCAD had the initial main section of the cone at 3.375" in length, and total length of 3.737" from the very front mullion.

Based on my own calcs, I estimate it to be slightly longer at just over 3.8" which is really a negligible difference. It's difficult to definitively say.

Like a lot of this stuff it's really a matter of 'He's got to choose his own path. No one can choose it for him.' sorta thing.

That quote was for my friend Searun 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

Click to expand...

Hey, I'll try not to bang on too much about it either.  I agree with trying to maintain the aspect ratio per se, and the look as you put it. 

Despite taking many pictures of the ESB 32"er, I'm not sure what the cockpit cone angle is as I haven't really studied it at all. It's a great miniature but it's no 5 footer. 

I think they originally had the angle of the 5' cone on the drawing pegged at around 18° from what I can see. I'd suggest between 18° to 19° is good and work with this.

I can't speak for their drawings as I had no input, but I would have thought that angles being referenced are pretty reliable, regardless of scale. So as the scale is manipulated, the angle will stay the same but the resultant length will slightly change proportionally to the diameter.

The difference between the 6" and 5.75" cone is only 4%. The V1 drawing at 3.737" in length, 4% longer is 3.88". If my math is correct...

So I feel it should be somewhere between the two. It comes down to your own workings out. A good in focus, straight on photo, from a medium distance is a good clue.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Just FYI, it's my strong contention that Part #998 is not a "custom-made part" but rather a unique assembly strategy of the already-known parts of this subassembly from the Y-Wing that also gets used on the Falcon. If you want to go lasercutting.. (or in general) You better learn 'Illustrator' then you are not working with pixels. But vector info.

Just drop your PSD in Illustrator... and workflow for tracing is way better in illustator. And if you would be off in measurements you could easier correct also.

Post #351  (forum post #5300445)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Mar 26, 2022 at 6:08 AM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Time for a little Saturday morning math regarding the mandible pits. This is what I’m thinking:

Mandible depth is 50.8mm total

Using 3mm plates for the mandible, that would be 50.8-6 for a total depth of 44.8mm to work with

Now if I use 3mm plates for the bottom of each pit that’s another 6mm for a remaining depth of 38.8

Two mandibles stacked 38.8/2= 19.4mm or 3/4” depth for each pit

What do you think?
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #352  (forum post #5300590)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Mar 26, 2022 at 5:49 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Bjorn
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			45.5” or 46” for the diameter of the hull? I’ve read both. I’m getting ready to make a paper template…if I can find paper wide enough. 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

Click to expand...

For me, the isometric on the internet (and in the old threads on this site) of the the disc, jaw box & mandible intersection was worth its weight in gold. I got lucky understanding that the hull was literally a discus and that height of the various ”walls”, less armor thickness, depended upon diameter.  

Now I appreciate the kit parts tell you these thicknesses at diameter 45.5” for the cut back up front and help insure proper thickness at the 47.5 disc overlap edge:

Sheridan for the jaw box inner mouth & Pontoon Assault Bridge for “naked“ mandible thickness defines what are actually channel thicknesses at diameter 45.5” & 47.5.

Good luck with your measurement decisions as you choose materials, determine construction for the build and factor in skin & armor thickness.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #354  (forum post #5301222)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Mar 28, 2022 at 3:43 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, swgeek, Studio Kitbash
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99,

Width is approximately the base the circle of the airlock cone which is 7.25“ on the drawing as you noticed.  Or, you can use the side elevation view centerline measurement of 3.688” X 2 per the Andre drawings. Wonderful work by his team. It may just be me, but some measurements may be “naked” and some may be “dressed”,  but the stack up of kit parts usually validate how you measure. Any big bust in my build is because I am going a little off script in what I desire by way of added features. Have fun. 

Searun

						95821 (VHD-600 / pack of 1)
					

CLEAR ACRYLIC DISHED DOMES Use for vertical and horizontal tank ends when making storage tanks for oil processing plants, water, LP gas or food and grain. Also popular in craft and home decor projects. Precision Injection Molded in Clear Acrylic or ABS Plastic.

[IMAGE: p_32x32.jpg]

					plastruct.com

Post #374  (forum post #5303082)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Apr 3, 2022 at 3:27 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Acrylic IS brittle, I can assure you!  I got both, because I really love the idea of having the acrylic one in order to see the other side as you apply the greeblies on the opposite side, in order to position everything just right.  But I got the ABS in case my milling out of the acrylic edge goes south. So it's mill first, THEN apply greeblies.  In Japan they have a phrase, "Kumano!" as in, "It's a bear."

Post #446  (forum post #5309967)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Apr 27, 2022 at 4:07 PM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99,

I am working on the sides of the inboard mandibles.  Do you have a clear picture of this port and starboard area or validated a map yet?

See attached from the internet. Once you get your head around the typical Falcon ”Through the Looking Glass“ questions of up and down with few things symmetrical, here is what has me stumped:

Is the drawing wrong regarding the the Pontoon Bridge orientation for the Starboard side?

On the Bandai 1/72 Perfect Grade model, the pontoon bridge horizontal slats (with port holes),  are on the “bottom” and the vertical picket fence slats are on “top” for both port and starboard Inboard side panels.

Thanks in advance assuming I have not confused myself.

 

Attachments

[IMAGE: 1212141-c490e11d8267e7141c1202d3ff20893a.jpg]

Searun, may I suggest using the 5' photo references in the various online photo albums as a guide. 

Whilst some of those drawing are beautiful and handy for overall details, I wouldn't necessarily trust them for referencing such things.

But again it depends on how bent obsessed you are over accuracy. 

Unfortunately I don't own a Bandai yet so I can't say either way.

You're at that FANTASY stage of construction where, if it were me, I'd start thinking about installing a Death Star Docking Bay ring as the transition from one room to the next, so that it's the 5-footer Falcon that actually "invites" you into the next room...

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1651599406093-png.1571656/]

It's your room, and your house, of course.  I'm just sayin' if I had a clean palette to work with, I'd think about architectural choices as reasons to build certain walls certain ways...  (I also think this concept, executed well, could be it's own private recess and "dust-free" storage area for the Falcon when not on display, as you could retract it, tilt it on its side, and then have a plexiglass screen come down like a window to cover it and protect it from dust.)

But I've been fantasizing about a larger house for over a decade now... so forgive me for projecting my dreams onto your room addition!

Post #500  (forum post #5313033)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : May 6, 2022 at 5:43 AM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			I just noticed that this is also in the  1/48 Jagd Panther as well. I don't have the Bandai 1/48 Panther on my list so I don't know if it's needed elsewhere, but if not the JP will suffice,

View attachment 1572747

Click to expand...

You need two Bandai 1/48 Panther G's to make the Falcon, one of which is to use the cut-down upper hull to go on lower front landing gear at 45 angle (with door piece #D3 installed), #A15 (x4, only 2 per kit) wheels on either side of quad guns - unmarked on maps, #C5 for upper mandible walkway front/center, #D9 for upper mandible walkway, #B9/B10 for lower underside split Centurion dressings – fender sections (trimmed down) drape off of Honda F1 engine halves L/R, #E23 and #D9/D10, #E40 (for upper jaw box)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #509  (forum post #5313679)
Author    : t2sides
Date      : May 8, 2022 at 10:42 AM
Reactions : Like — Searun, Bjorn
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FWIW - Depending on the 5 Footer you are building, the Front Cover plate Orientation is different.

If you are going for Original Filiming, A New Hope Replica, be sure you get the Ferrari Cover plates in the right orientation.  The B&W Photos in Chronicles and current model orientations are NOT as they were during Filming and as seen on film.   Here's a quick reference for orientation if you are going ANH Style.

Below Starboard Mandible Front, B&W on left, you will see the Orientation as the cover plate was AFTER ANH filming (and in Chronicles), and the orientation on the right as it was actually filmed (my replica - I've since replaced the incorrect yellow peeling decal 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/0-anh-cover-plate-stb-jpg.1573404/]

Below, Port Mandible Front, B&W shows again this cover plate is 180 degrees out since Filming, the one on the right shows orientation as it was filmed.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/0-coverplateport-jpg.1573406/]

Below, Screen Shot from Death Star docking scene, showing the actual orientation and matching the ones on the right above.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/0-cover-1-jpg.1573405/]

The other telltale sign are those valve openings, you can see in the screencap and my photos the valve openings are closer to the 'inside mandible' areas, and on the Ref Photos, they are farther away from the inner mandible, showing the 180 degree orientation off-set.

Hope that helps for those building ANH version.

-Sean
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Learned something about this sneaky little guy today. Almost all of the Tamiya cars have this part. However...only one is right. 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]
 The "Elf" Tyrrell Ford F-1 (003) 1971 Tamiya | No. 12009 | 1:12 is the only one that doesn't have a hole in the end. Here it is next to the McLaren part.

ELF                                                                                                                                                                     

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1652465017693-png.1575092/]

 

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1652465075131-png.1575093/]

McLaren

Post #553  (forum post #5315373)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : May 13, 2022 at 3:08 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Learned something about this sneaky little guy today. Almost all of the Tamiya cars have this part. However...only one is right. 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]
 The "Elf" Tyrrell Ford F-1 (003) 1971 Tamiya | No. 12009 | 1:12 is the only one that doesn't have a hole in the end. Here it is next to the McLaren part.

ELF                                                                                                                                                                    

View attachment 1575092 View attachment 1575093McLaren

View attachment 1575086

Click to expand...

The C-sprue or engine sprue from the Tamiya 1/12 F1 cars was altered sometime i believe in the late 70's. There are notable differences in some parts from between the vintage & newer issues. ILM of course used the vintage F1 kits which were plentiful at the time, the Texaco McLaren & Tyrrell Ford F1 being the dominant two kits used. So to the purist only the orignal issue is sought after & used & we know how much these kits go for now...ouch!.

I lucked out years ago on Ebay with a seller having like 5 vintage C-sprues for sale for something ridiculously cheap & haven't needed to buy too many complete kits for my build.

But, annoyingly enough, not all of the so called vintage F1 kits have the original C-sprue. I & a few others have found this out, it can be a hit & miss kind of deal. What i do is ask the sellers to provide clear pics of the C-sprue to determine if true vintage or not. The part you found is a good 'tell' of which is which.

The altered C-sprue is not as sharp in molding either, a little soft, especially when compared to the vintage. The newer issues with the photoetch are even softer in molding.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #585  (forum post #5317565)
Author    : Bjorn
Date      : May 19, 2022 at 10:57 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, swgeek
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

bwayne64 said:

			Acrylic is a pain in the tookus, IMHO. But that is the Studio Model way. If you have a laser cutter it's a piece of cake. Any other way and I'd have to join hair club for Men , ; )
		

Click to expand...

If it's on a CNC router, try to limit the rpm and keep the feed up. You want nice small chips coming off the cutter.

Fine snowflakes usually means the RPM is too fast. And will soon weld itself to the router bit.

RPM too slow and your about to break something. Either the cutter, or the acrylic, or both.

That said it's better to start with slower rpm and medium feed than high rpm and a molten mess around the cutter.

If it's a manual router, may the force be with you!
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Can we talk pipes?

So I have a chance before the end of the school year to have the metal shop kids weld the cross section/mounting pipes. Is 2.25 diameter "x 46 the go to for the cross pipe? My goal is to follow others and mount it to an engine stand while I work on it and then mount it from the bottom when it's finished. What would you recommend for the vertical mounting dimensions?

Thanks!

John
		

Click to expand...

This is something I posted in the Vfxup64 thread. I'm thinking of using this at the center of the armature. Lined up with the gun turret windows and the escape pods. They come in different tube diameters. I think the 1 3/8 " tubes are more than strong enough. Especially since they tie into the body at several points. You can also cut the tubes down if they're too long. Just an idea. I want a 6 way mounting armature. Even if I don't use them all. You'll need an adapter to mount to an engine stand, as the tube there is larger.  You probably won't need the eye hooks, ; )

[IMAGE: 6_WAY__33242__93402.1568928714.386.513.png]

						1 3/8" 6-Way Flat Roof Center
					

Great deals on PTMs wide selection of Galvanized Steel Canopy Connectors for flat and peaked canopy frame, available to fit different pipe sizes.

[IMAGE: PTM%20Favicon.png]

					ptmtarps.com
				

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/6-way-canopy-fitting-png.1577764/]

Post #612  (forum post #5318096)

jcoffman99 said:

			My apologies. Certainly was not my intent. I’ll slow my roll.

John
		

Click to expand...

I was going to let this slide, but the more i think about it I'll say my piece here, especially as I see Sean has the same feelings.

To be given help with a few ID's here & there is one thing, but to be given a parts map such as Tsophika's is the 'ultimate' gesture & help. But then to say it is inaccurate is bad form!. 

I'll accept that you hadn't realised the importance of this gesture, folks that come into the Studio Scale world & start IDing & blueprinting for themselves don't realise the hard work & years of research that has been done before such as this Falcon miniature. 

A parts map represents more over not only the authors work, but the work of many, years of pain & searching for just 'that' annoying part that can be eluding. 

For us few that have been down the Falcon road, it is a long road i will say, i think 2011when i came into it & already the knowledge base was vast on the ID's, i learnt i had to be patient & not demanding of the amazing guys that had the wealth of knowledge i sought!. It's about building relationships & trust & also doing alot of the work yourself. 

Proof of build goes a long way i might add to garner help & trust & that is how i think for myself to help others, I'm not keen on simply handing out what is essentially years & years of material.

In a nutshell then...respect others work, what they are giving out, what that material actually means that you now have that will literally save you years of work. The Studio Scale world is a beast!. It sucks you in, more so when you get in deep. The Falcon is a huge project & never a one person effort in its entirety from start to finish. 

Stu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I totally get it Stu. I’ve reached out to Craig to apologize. It never even entered my mind how I was coming across. I totally see it, but please know it wasn’t my intent. I was going off of this map so I really thought I was helping. This is why I’m trying to organize all this stuff to weed out the inconsistencies between maps.  But I totally get it. My brain tends to go very fast. I’m trying to slow down.

I’ve had several people reach out and thank me for what I’m doing, and I always tell them I’m not really doing anything, that you guys did the heavy lifting.  Again, sorry all,

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Thanks, all. John did reach out and we're good.

I set out to create some 'definitive' part maps to learn as much about the model as I could. The nail was hit on the head: they are a cumulation of a dozen-plus people's eyes, efforts and expenses. And an ever-evolving project as new research is made, so if you do actually find an error, let me know!

I have spent hundreds of hours on this subject myself, even discovered a couple kits here and there, but I was saved literal decades of work by others who reached out to help early on. Friends to this day. And of course Sean and Stu's work speaks for itself.

Few too make it past the collecting stage, so talk of armatures and materials is exciting to see. The discussion in this thread is very much in the spirit of what attracted me to the hobby and I very much look forward to seeing some more 4' Falcons! (ILM called it the 4' model.)

Craig
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #624  (forum post #5318731)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : May 23, 2022 at 6:14 PM
Reactions : Like, Love — Joseph C. Brown, bwayne64
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Okay. Test run #1 on the turret base complete. What did we learn?

1. Bandsaw works great for roughing out the shape.

2.  Dremel with sanding bit is not going to cut it. Not enough control.

3. Benchtop belt sander seemed to work pretty well.

4. Eye protection…very important.

As always, criticism is welcome.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/7cc9fe3e-cfb5-4878-bb3f-3d563459f0b7-jpeg.1578399/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Are you going to wrap the edge with styrene. Or just sand the bloody heck out of it, ; ) The layer lines from my 3d prints are making me really want a resin printer. Well that and it's a new toy, LOL. I'm building an enclosure to print Asa filament. I hear you can vapor smooth this like Abs and Asa has better uv resistance. Don't plan on having it in the sun, but everything helps. I think once you hit that with a few coats of filler primer, you can probably glue the greeblies right to it. Just rambling, had too much sugar today, ; )

All right -- here's my pictures, here's my take:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1653858320523-jpeg.1580734/]

C29 compared to my two best imitations of the original subassembly.  C29 sits on top of the third (far right) version, which currently has no bumps added to it.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1653858385230-jpeg.1580735/]

C29 sitting in the same surface plane in comparison to DaveG's 3D-modeled version of the subassembly, showing a high similarity.

My take:  not a match.  My reasons are threefold:  1.) bumps too close together, 2.) width of greeblie too narrow to match any version of the area, 3.) length of C29 greeblie not long enough to "fill" that front section of the "missing greeblie" ID area.

I am of course very willing to be wrong, and have been wrong multiple times in the past.  And it is certainly, to my knowledge, the best "existing greeblie" guess we've seen so far on this particular mystery.

Post #701  (forum post #5323907)
Author    : Bjorn
Date      : Jun 5, 2022 at 5:14 AM
Reactions : Like — bwayne64, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			So I’m getting ready to cut my Patty shapes. Can someone please double check me here before I cut?  Thank you!

View attachment 1582843

Click to expand...

John, I'm surprised that you are taking on the patty. I know it is probably the first piece of structure that you want to build and square away, but the way I look at it, you need to be confident in your overall size. And going by your question it seems that your not sure.

And with this in mind it is best to do your own work here as everything is interconnected. And you should be satisfied that everything else marries up.

When I look at Eagle1's super build thread, he started laying out the individual sections with kit parts and matching to photo references, like the upper and lower jaw box or docking ring arms. These help give clue to the real proportions. And he took several goes at it. It will unlikely be right first time.

This seems to be the traditional approach and a very good one. Whilst I have seen very little about it, I presume Mr Lee Malone did the same and the result is nothing short of amazing.

Only this way can you possibly start to determine the inner structure.

The easiest way is to lay out some styrene and start mocking it up, much the same as Stu has done. Use the kit parts as your guide. 

Trying to start with the centre patty is a big leap of faith. Very rewarding if you get it right but otherwise....

I have spent a few years already on this process and I'm still a while a way from physically cutting something with confidence.

Per Searun's build, Andre and Joshua's drawings are a great starting place. 

I have been trying to determine the dimensions of the core / patty for the last few months and I'm still unsure of several aspects that I am keen to resolve.

My current findings are not to diffrent from your detailed sketch. 

I make the hull to be slightly larger at about 46.5" OD, 40.5 - 41" between docking rings, and shy of 7.1" across the docking ring face. And that 110° is probably closer to 108°. Difficult to tell with the armour plating. 

Face it, if it was easy, there would be more of these things. As it's not just the fastest ship in the galaxy but the coolest hands down. Or Hans...

Do your homework, it will pay off.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

And if you're lucky, you won't get to add another year to the build process like I did, simply by having one dimension wrong and having to start all over again!

But it is very cool looking, and gets the imagination firing, so congrats on committing to a number and pulling the trigger.  Can't wait to see the build start to come together.

BTW, what was the O.D. number you settled on?
		

Click to expand...

Lol. I joked that it would have taken me hours and would have ended up looking like a triangle if I had to cut it by hand.

I'm sure I'll have many mistakes/rebuilds/crying fits. Isn't that part of the fun? 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

I went with 46" We'll see...

Post #728  (forum post #5326259)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Jun 11, 2022 at 9:24 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Studio Kitbash
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I built the PG pits today and took some measurements. I also took pics to give a 360 degree view of the pits. I added all of them to the Bandai Scans folder mentioned above.

These are the interior measurements I came up with from one side of the pit to the other.

Starboard Bottom Front Pit 24.64mm x 3.51152 = 86.5238528mm = 3.4 in

Starboard Bottom Rear Pit 25.30mm x 3.51152 =  88.841456 = 3.5 in

Starboard Top Front Pit 24.67mm x 3.51152 = 86.6291984 mm = 3.4 in

Starboard Top Rear Pit 25.39mm x 3.51152 = 89.1574928 mm = 3.5 in

Port Bottom Front Pit 24.95mm x 3.51152 = 87.612424 mm = 3.5 in

Port Bottom Rear Pit 25.62mm x 3.51152= 89.9651424 mm = 3.5 in

Port Top Front Pit 24.64mm x 3.51152 = 86.5238528mm = 3.4 in

Port Top Rear Pit 25.22mm x 3.51152 = 88.5605344 = 3.5 in
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I've used simple green cleaner to soak parts. Sometimes it will break the glue bond. You might want to test it on a part you don't need. Putting it in the freezer works sometimes too. Good luck,

; )

Tried making some copies of the Harrow pieces. I’m having trouble getting the very tops of the taller pieces. I tried using a toothpick to get the air out and the resin in deeper and it didn’t quite do the trick. Any suggestions?

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/3b8e5ddb-5271-4e95-a91d-154319c00736-jpeg.1624452/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #763  (forum post #5364317)
Author    : joberg
Date      : Oct 7, 2022 at 6:41 AM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I'm sure others are going to chime in...but the toothpicks are not a bad trick if you leave them inside and then pour your resin...then when the mold is full, remove gently the toothpicks from the holes.

If it's a quick resin, then the trick doesn't work anymore
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]
 Which kind are you using?
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Oct 7, 2022 at 6:50 AM

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #765  (forum post #5364426)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Oct 7, 2022 at 1:23 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99, swgeek
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Cast the resin in a pressure pot under 60psi and you won't have this problem -- pushes all the bubbles to the top of the mold, which is the bottom of the part.  If any base/surface bubbles are annoying, you can fill them with putty.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #791  (forum post #5366800)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Oct 15, 2022 at 7:30 AM
Reactions : Like, Wow — swgeek, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The Morser Karl came out in 1976, and I would be very surprised if that greeblie was an ESB refit -- here's why:  I'm fairly confident that this piece was "actually" (i.e., not symbolically or aesthetically) an air vent to allow outside air to be pulled into the model which would then get sucked through the muffin fans to cool off the inside during the halogen light filming scenes.  There are a few reference pictures that seem to indicate as much, showing a very dark recess underneath the grill that suggests it is not only a greeblie but an actual "working element" of the model.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Yeah, I am not in front of my kits right now, but in my notes it says either/or, as if the vent greeblie is available in both kits, and I don't actually remember, but either way I'd be keen to have your opinion on my theory that this vent greeblie actually covered a real hole that was directly underneath the upper engine vents, to allow for air to pass through the model from bottom to top, in addition to the airflow from front to back of the ship, via the Koolshade covering the holes around the cockpit tube, which would also let a lot of air in.

Post #795  (forum post #5366892)
Author    : t2sides
Date      : Oct 15, 2022 at 1:04 PM
Reactions : Like — Searun, joberg, Studio Kitbash
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FWIW - the last two parts you've been talking about were Definitively ESB add-ons, neither that gas can like part or the round vent were present for ANH.  (one of the best public references is the Color Crayola box pic (in Chronicles) - pretty much nails those parts not there).   Remember, the Chronicles B&W pics were taken AFTER filming of ANH - so you're finding a number of parts that were post ANH when you use those references.   Just depends which Falcon you want to build.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Oh, interesting!  That picture doesn't "totally" satisfy or prove the point, but it satisfies my curiosity, and certainly makes the case that it was not used as an air vent hole, which, if it was, I would think the "insert" part would be snugged flush to the body panel, whereas this pic shows it sitting proud of the surface as if there is nothing underneath it but solid plastic.  That's a nice angle, and very helpful.  

Sean, thanks too for your insights -- I didn't know this was definitely an ESB refit, as I had presumed the "origin date" of the kit argument was the only factor.  I wonder "why" they added it?  Is it possible there was a hole there (for airflow), and that they covered it up with the round vent greeblie for an underside filming shot (the Bespin take-off/landing sequence?).  Because it doesn't seem like either a "blank area that needs filling" or like a "greeblie that fell off needs replacing" area, but again I don't know what it looked like before, during ANH, unless it was simply a blank piece of sytrene in this area.

Post #810  (forum post #5368126)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Oct 19, 2022 at 9:03 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, swgeek, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Okay, JCoffman is correct -- the correct greeblies are ONLY on the Fujimi 1/76 German Troop Carrier -- they are NOT in the Esci "Rocket Launcher" (which you also need) kit.

The difference is in the pieces and in the part numbers.  The correct part numbers are #45/47, whereas the wrong part numbers (on the Esci kit) are #58/59.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1666195180272-jpeg.1628889/]

Correct Fujimi parts #45/47 on the left - longer front, raised nibs above each axle, and front/rear angle differences.

Incorrect Esci parts #58/59 on the right:  8 axles, similar color, similar shape, and yet not the same at all.

Thanks for the eagle eyes, JCoffman!

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1666195330742-jpeg.1628890/]

So, to reiterate:  Left = Right, and Right = Wrong

Clear?
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #826  (forum post #5373675)

Date      : Nov 6, 2022 at 7:51 AM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Okay. Test run #1 on the turret base complete. What did we learn?

1. Bandsaw works great for roughing out the shape.

2.  Dremel with sanding bit is not going to cut it. Not enough control.

3. Benchtop belt sander seemed to work pretty well.

4. Eye protection…very important.

As always, criticism is welcome.

View attachment 1578399

Click to expand...

Auto body putty is your friend. There is also a brushable filler for 3d prints on the market. it self levels.

Yea I can relate. My problem is getting good polyflow across objects. Sometimes they get real funky. Be careful, 3d building can be addictive, like map making, ; )

If you ever have trouble with a particular shape, this site is helpful. The first pages will be missing photos, as they are ancient, but there's tons of good stuff here.

[IMAGE: gky4zpdnt5xx.jpg]

						How The F*#% Do I Model This? - Reply for help with specific shapes - (Post attempt before asking)
					

Alright, so i see time and time again that people tend have trouble doing certain things with hard-shapes and sub-d modeling.

[IMAGE: favicon_2057333fd190ab0eaa48285b1ebc0234.ico]

Post #857  (forum post #5375854)
Author    : bwayne64
Date      : Nov 12, 2022 at 10:00 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Yea I use the bezier curve function in LW. It can be finicky as well. Another thing I do on a shape like that is draw out a disk to match the curve and then drop a few points below to make the angled sides. It's easier to do than explain, lol. But LW is a poly modeler not surface. So it's probably a whole different animal.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #863  (forum post #5376100)
Author    : bwayne64
Date      : Nov 13, 2022 at 7:46 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

That's one of the great things about virtual objects, if they aren't right you don't have to break out the knives and saws, lol. I don't know what type of statistics your program has, but I always have to check for stray points and non planer poly's. Usually your slicer will throw out an error message. But you probably know this. As small as most kit parts are, at least we're not losing a lot of filament or resin when we goof up.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #872  (forum post #5376748)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Nov 15, 2022 at 5:44 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Okay so I’ve been working on the docking rings and looking at the existing blueprints I can’t seem to calculate these dimensions. Have they been nailed down officially? Any suggestions? I’ll make official drawings/3D files available if I can get them nailed down.

View attachment 1636133

Click to expand...

jcoffnan99,

For what it’s worth, a micrometer carefully used on the 1/72 Bandai Falcon is a good check when dimensions are in doubt or unknown and kit part bench marks do not help. I use the 3.511 multiplier developed by one of the RPF veterans. 

I have found that one needs to be constant in rounding off. The nearest 1/32 inch works for me with 1/64 the best my eyes can do for small parts.

Those very small measurements are tricky. Measuring a critical item a few times helps assure accuracy. Be aware of “stack up” when measuring a string of item lengths separately or selecting material thickness.

I measure constantly during my build to check construction as-built, edge distances, those larger armor segment gaps and recently even on surface ”chips” to get proportions reasonably correct. 

Best of luck.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #873  (forum post #5376785)
Author    : swgeek
Date      : Nov 15, 2022 at 8:22 PM
Reactions : Like — Searun, joberg

jcoffman99 said:

			So what’s your preferred way for cutting large pieces like this? Imma scared. 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

Click to expand...

My method is a Dremel cut off wheel, making sure I cut the part too big and then slowly sand it to the correct shape & size.

Post #906  (forum post #5378342)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Nov 21, 2022 at 10:24 AM
Reactions : Like — randalls88, Searun, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			The last piece I'm looking for is the part underneath the Sherman in red. It says It's the motorized Tamiya M4 Sherman, but I can't find a kit scan to confirm. Thoughts?

Thanks,

John

View attachment 1640642

Click to expand...

It's an upper hull grill section, cut out and then cast/replicated, which only the vintage Tamiya 1/35 M4A3E8 Sherman gives you the right dimensions on.  You then cut these in half, meaning you get 2 of the 8 greeblies you need from the original.  You also need part #A11 from this kit for the upper mandala section - here too you only get one per kit, and you need two of them, for port and starboard side, and so you have to make a casting.

Four Sherman grill castings are stacked vertically in the center of this picture -- you cut them in "more than" half and discard the center section, and you have the 8 pieces needed for the Docking Rings:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1669055006454-jpeg.1640652/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #941  (forum post #5380549)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Nov 28, 2022 at 9:46 AM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So here’s a question about hull plating. What’s your go to method for measuring and cutting curves? Let’s take this panel for example:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/b65046e8-7df2-402e-ad86-0f979a1f6124-png.1642725/]

How do you get the perfect curve?
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #942  (forum post #5380602)
Author    : swgeek
Date      : Nov 28, 2022 at 1:56 PM
Reactions : Like — mb1k, Treadwell, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

For pieces like this I print them out on paper first, cut them out with scissors and make sure they fit. Then I use that template to mark the real piece of plastic, cut it out with an x-acto and then sand the edges with an emery board.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #943  (forum post #5380611)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Nov 28, 2022 at 2:31 PM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

swgeek said:

			For pieces like this I print them out on paper first, cut them out with scissors and make sure they fit. Then I use that template to mark the real piece of plastic, cut it out with an x-acto and then sand the edges with an emery board.
		

Click to expand...

Awesome. That’s what I was thinking.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #944  (forum post #5380612)
Author    : TazMan2000
Date      : Nov 28, 2022 at 2:31 PM
Reactions : Like — mb1k, joberg, swgeek
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

In addition to what swgeek said, you may want to cut them slightly larger so that you can sand or cut the edges for a smoother curve until you get used to measuring, cutting and fitting. You can always fix a larger panel by trimming, but gluing a millimeter strip to where you trimmed too much will look terrible and will take more time to fix.

TazMan2000
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #945  (forum post #5380615)
Author    : TazMan2000
Date      : Nov 28, 2022 at 2:37 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Also, since there is a slight curve, it may be difficult to get a flat piece of plastic to look good when glued down. What I did when gluing plates of plastic onto a sphere was cut slices in the middle of the piece, being careful not to go end to end so that they would split. The "cuts" would open up slightly, but allow the panel to sit flat on a curved surface. Once glued, fill in the "cuts".

TazMan2000
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #946  (forum post #5380655)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Nov 28, 2022 at 6:00 PM
Reactions : Like — mb1k, Searun, Ogibendogy
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Thought I'd put my 2 cents worth here on panel cutting.

Guys, there is no issue with the styrene curved panels sitting on the hull flat!. Because they do. Even the bigger panels sit perfectly on the hull, believe me!. As they are curved, they match the long length of curvature. There's no issue to be had here ok.

As for my personal panel cutting preference, I use thin card templates on the trickier panels & transfer to the styrene sheet to cut out the panel proper.

If you make a mistake on an edge, as I have done on quite a few, then you cut off a goodly strip, say more than 10mm & butt up a new piece of styrene & sand to it is as one again & continue. It's pointless in most cases to fabricate an entirely new panel.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #947  (forum post #5380656)
Author    : jcoffman99

Post #962  (forum post #5381403)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Dec 1, 2022 at 7:23 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

After my simpleton method at the beginning of the build, I remembered how we took critical “chord” measurements when doing alignment work.

Although a trammeling tool is great for aircraft wing rib spacing, it makes perfect circles when a large drafting tool is needed. Helps on the very unforgiving engine deck due to kit part grid lock.

 

Attachments

[IMAGE: 1286037-2dd4f0313940c2fd7eae7be82fb4a71e.jpg]

B48443F9-B691-4BD6-9845-A70413145EEE.jpeg

					1.7 MB

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			So is the larger, round piece under the Ferrari piece  from the McLaren? I only have a few pieces left on this sidewall.

View attachment 1644415View attachment 1644416

Click to expand...

If correct, that's a really good discovery -- if it is a double stack, it's significant because it's part #7 in both cases, and b/c it's another reason you need the vintage Marlboro M23 instead of the newer Yardley McLaren 23 -- D7 on that kit is the top cap to the elusive two screw-driver half pieces, that you don't get in the later Yardley boxings.  

Got any angle shots that "prove" it's that tall?  Without having the pieces on hand to place against the sidewall, it looks like a stack might make it "too tall" and stand proud of the chassis rail above it by too much, but I'm only speculating.  The image on the left you've posted certainly makes it "look" like it might be two concentric pieces stacked on top of each other, but that could also just be a trick of the light, photography, or paint job.  

Here's the closest I could find, and it is certainly suggestive that it might be taller than just the Ferrari piece:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1670165639236-jpeg.1644533/]

Either way, it's great sleuthing you're doing, and easily the most fun Easter egg hunt in the galaxy...
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I'm still not in my studio with pieces to compare, but I'd say that last picture (far right) certainly nails the argument:  it's clear that the piece on top has been placed "off-center" onto the piece below it, and if that piece isn't D7 from the M23, well it sure looks like it!  And since the rest of that screwdriver shows up just a few greeblies to the left, they were clearly working with Sprue D on that section of the ship.  So I think you've nailed it.

John, I have to say it's nice that you got yourself one of those but do you realise how rare these are becoming?

Those that have gone before us have respectfully chosen not to openly show these rare kits so blatantly.

And I'm sure it's not because of one-up-manship, but to try and keep a lid on prices. Be it the avid model kit collector or the Studio Scale modeller.

There are surely other ways of going about it.

Post #1120  (forum post #5386387)
Author    : swgeek
Date      : Dec 17, 2022 at 8:53 PM
Reactions : Like, Love — joberg, James Kenobi 1138, Treadwell
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I understand not wanting to drive prices up, I really do, but that ship has sailed. My TIE-Fighter has cost me more than $8,000 to build and I'm not done. If you think you're going to get into studio scale model making without spending gobs of money, I've got bad news for you. Just the Sealab alone is going north of $1000 and it's in every A New Hope model. Or the Bandai Panther G, when I bought my first one it was $35 now they go for $75. I get it, I really do, none of us want to see this hobby get any more exspensive than it already is but I'm not sure how we're supposed to know which model kits we can talk about and which we can't. I don't want to piss anybody off, especially all the people that have really helped me over the years. I couldn't make any of the models I do without the help and support of this community. I know we've all dealt with a lot of leaches over the years, "tell me all the kits". Hell, I've been guilty of that in my early days but I think we need to cut each other some slack, especially for those who have actually contributed to this community.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1121  (forum post #5386479)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Dec 18, 2022 at 6:38 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, JSchneider, Searun
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

WHO's got Bandai 1/48 Panther G's for $75???

Tell them to call me -- I'm in the market, and I've been paying $125.

But yeah, pricey hobby for sure!  You guys see the Bandai 1/24 M60 that went for $1800 last month?  THAT was a new record.

Thus, my proposed solution:  digital scans of everything.  But admittedly, the joy of seeing a resin print come off the printer is NOT the same as seeing an old rare kit arrive in the mail, or show up at a swap meet, or found in a basement estate sale, so the archeological treasure-hunt aspect of it is, for me at least, a huge part of the fun and challenge.

Post #1125  (forum post #5386615)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Dec 18, 2022 at 2:31 PM
Reactions : Like, Sad — joberg, bwayne64
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			This kit was already posted in another build thread, so I didn’t know there was an issue. Someone really should write a rule book because everybody seems to have their own.
		

Click to expand...

I'm going to say my thoughts on this issue of the rarer kits & an unstated etiquette among Studio Scale modelers that I can see just isn't there as it used to be.

To my mind it's all a hot political type arena & I tend to stay out of it & remain neutral to a point, but the ID's of the rarer kits are a hot topic among Falcon builders.

Rules??. Well no, there aren't any, not written anyway. But...there is etiquette & thought that goes into the publication of the rare kits.

These ID's go a long way back to their finders, the old guard so to speak of the Studio Scale builders. They kept these ID's off the boards for a few reasons, but over the years a few have made it out there into the world, but still held close to serious builders of the Falcon & other subjects.

The main reason of not making publicly known on boards etc... is that of price hiking & availability. Aa soon as a rare kit, such as the one you have now shown is made public, sellers somehow get to know the worth of their kit & bump up the price...dramatically I might add!. And so do others that have it & make for sale. It's a thing!. It has been seen in many many kits.

So in general, regarding etiquette in the Studio Scale world & with budding Falcon builders as here, we tend to keep the ID's close, not out in the open. If an ID is told to someone, it's usually explained to them to please keep off the boards. This being the only 'rule'...but not a rule...hence you see the politics of it all.

Over time, many years of doing this, only then does one realise the importance of one's actions.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

And so do others that have it & make for sale. It's a thing!. It has been seen in many many kits.

So in general, regarding etiquette in the Studio Scale world & with budding Falcon builders as here, we tend to keep the ID's close, not out in the open. If an ID is told to someone, it's usually explained to them to please keep off the boards. This being the only 'rule'...but not a rule...hence you see the politics of it all.

Over time, many years of doing this, only then does one realise the importance of one's actions.
		

Click to expand...

Stu thanks for your perspective. My only argument is that kit was already posted and anyone could see exactly what kit it went to.  Maybe we need a “rare kit” list passed in secret so we all know what the “rare” kits are. I didn’t think the price was out of line for any kit so it didn’t lead me to believe it was that rare. 

I fear that I’ll never understand the studio scale world.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1127  (forum post #5386644)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Dec 18, 2022 at 4:39 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, bwayne64
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

It's a hard hobby to understand i know, because that's what it is ultimately. Took me years to get to grips with the unspoken 'rules' to it, but over time one learns how to be at one with it so to speak. 

The price gouging on certain kits has increased over the years, but this is due to the fact the kits have become known, sellers increase the price & the kits themselves 'dry up' in availability & also folks hoard these rare kits when found & have no intention of ever using them in a build. 

Already alot of the kits cannot be found easily, sometimes years in an example coming up for sale. 

It's going to be much tougher down the road to build a Falcon, the 'old school' way, with actual donor kit parts from the vintage kits. But then, there aren't that many going down that route now with printing everything becoming the desired build material.

Don't fret though John, just have fun with your IDing thread & your build, kit collecting, because that's what it's all about...having fun!.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This lower jaw mouth is ESB based on head lights and other “ Lower Lip Stick & Botox” unless I miss understand internet pictures that call out the well known various ANH vs. ESB.

That said, I need to confirm 3 other differences that I have noticed myself and have not found these specifically called out on the internet other than noticing picture differences. 

1. I agree the circled Panther A boxes are not ANH.

2. Some castings available and a casting picture shown on the internet have the port Centurion fitting 180 degrees reversed on the lower lip.

3. Some maps on the internet appear to show one Jagdtiger cylinder and some show two on the “Sheridan” tank body mouth.  All these appear to be for the ESB version. Perhaps one is missing on the original Studio Scale from pictures. In any case, the ANH Bandai model has one and some pictures of other models show one for ANH.

Your thoughts on the above?

Post #1147  (forum post #5388030)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Dec 23, 2022 at 11:42 AM
Reactions : Haha, Like — joberg, Rick Deckard, bwayne64
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

One rule of thumb I've learned in six years of posting is what I call Rule Number One:

1.)  Never post a claim if you don't have the part in your physical possession to compare with neighboring parts.  It's way too easy (and I fell for it often) to get super-excited about a matching shape that you see on a Parts Tree scan, or an Ebay listing, and then pre-emptively make a declaration that turns out to be a red herring.  Get excited, then buy the kit, then check, and then make the claim.  It takes longer, is more expensive, but builds trust/credibility much better.  It's also when you DO do your homework first, and "prove" it's a match, that others who have gone before you will typically come out of hiding and say, "Psst, that looks good, but it's the wrong part -- the right part is this one" and I've been helped out of my own "certainty" more than once on things like this by showing my work.

2.) The Second Rule I Always Follow:  If you're a man, never put the phrase, "I've been told that it is too small" in any post, ever, anywhere, about anything....  ; )  

(I mean, c'mon, you did kind of walk yourself right into that one...)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I don't see any issue keeping certain details secret, publicly, and only sharing with actual falcon builders.

When it comes to rare items they have to be protected from stashers and hoarders. There's a lot of folk out there with more money than sense in my experience that just stash these items away if they get their hands on them and do nothing with them simply because they are rare and sort after and don't plan to do anything with them.. and there's plenty more of these folk than actual falcon builders. 

So I say only share info on those rare items with actual Falconers.

Your disc core measure is fine, that has to be maximum diameter though, accounting for sidewall thickness.

It's your mandible length then that's the issue.

I just saw your post back there where you had around 7" from mandible/sidewall end to docking ring sidewall edge. That's too short, like 2" at least.

Post #1208  (forum post #5394549)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Jan 15, 2023 at 3:41 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99, swgeek
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

It's really just a matter of tweaking for everything to gel.

With kit part layout on key areas like the mandible sidewalls, you can transfer your findings to a card template & use this as a 'wrap' to find out how accurate or far off you are from mandible tip to docking ring sidewall end!.. I'm so old school...but it works.

Post #1216  (forum post #5394577)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Jan 15, 2023 at 5:32 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Jcoffman99,

Great idea with the side wall jig, my build had some minor “sine wave” action from edge centerline. 

Make sure you check wall height using the appropriate kit part regardless of location. Disc edge is critical for FALGAL waist when skin & armor thickness add up top & bottom. Same for Sheridan at the jaw box mouth and both inboard and outboard mandible walls and even the engine flaps. I found nothing is the same and extra care was needed. A little extra as-built tolerance helps.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1217  (forum post #5394773)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Jan 16, 2023 at 11:09 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Studio Kitbash, Bjorn
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I need to add the bottom landing gear plateau to the list of vertical wall height cautions previously mentioned. At the correct elevation, this horizontal plane blends into the disc curvature just before the lower turret to disc joint. 

Note the Centurion hull fits on the diagonal wall slope. It acts as a good bench mark, but leave some elevation for the greeblies that ride on top of it. Have fun.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #1222  (forum post #5397020)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So when you need to bend a part to fit a curvature, is a heat gun overkill? Will a hairdryer be enough? Is there a better way?
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1223  (forum post #5397036)
Author    : swgeek
Date      : Jan 24, 2023 at 7:33 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Depends, if it's styrene a hair dryer maybe enough. If it's resin you'll need a heat gun. Even with a heat gun, if you're far enough away from the part you can regulate temperature pretty well.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #1229  (forum post #5400143)
Author    : satazius
Date      : Feb 3, 2023 at 1:45 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The parts on the Turbo laser are from the Hasegawa Sherman. If you look at the Turbo Laser model the open voids are filled-in and there is kind of odd damage. I believe that is because they are cast and not the kit parts themselves. And we know there were a number of castings made of the master pattern (a part of which you cited above), as cut-up parts show up repeatedly on the Falcon and trench of the ANH SD. So it would make sense they would be lying around and available. The Sherman parts are at one end of the master and easily cut off. And since they are clayed-in for casting they dont have the odd openings that would be present if using the original kit parts. So... motive and opportunity, LOL.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/screen-shot-2023-02-03-at-4-37-41-pm-jpg.1666222/]

What do you guys use to adhere your parts for casting. I've been using double-sided tape with mixed results.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1260  (forum post #5403266)
Author    : swgeek
Date      : Feb 13, 2023 at 11:18 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I CA my parts to foamcore. It works really well for me. The parts are nice and flat and all I have to sand off is a thin layer of paper.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #1262  (forum post #5403638)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Feb 14, 2023 at 1:41 PM
Reactions : Like — TazMan2000, joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

White (Elmer's) school glue in teeny amounts, cleaned up with Q-tip swabs and alcohol for any excess/spillover.

Hey John... Going through these Nichimo's today gave me an idea. Maybe a good one, maybe a bad one? Haha! I'm pretty sure the springs from the Honda CB750 are used somewhere, although I haven't confirmed that yet myself (Didn't I see a spring inner Mandible, Starboard? I don't remember atm. There's one somewhere.) So they were using these kit parts to their advantage we all assume, and it is the logical thing to do. Use what you have. Couldn't other parts from these donor kits be used as well? 

I'm not sure how much time they put into the little things while building it originally, but looking at the motor mounts for the Centurion's today gave me a scratch my head and "I wonder?" moment. Could they have done something like this:

[IMAGE: 1316573-d30ae997a2255cc1140d163af7baa278.jpg]

Maybe not from this kit, but another kit from the build? Or another part we haven't come across yet?

The metal is just shy of 1/32" thick, so it is pretty thin, and so is the bracket in that photo. There are already holes drilled into it. But the brackets would need to be cut out of the entire assembly. I don't know if they did this, but it I might have done it myself if I needed something in a pinch. But then again, I'm not that bright and the hardware store is just 5 minutes from my house! And I to this point haven't seen too many brackets, if any that thin. I don't know where that photo you posted is from location wise yet, so just judging scale by the photo itself.

[IMAGE: 1316582-93a536cd88cdeda51b83d7a5bf877de1.jpg]

What does everyone think? I can keep my mouth shut, but I can't turn my brain off! Haha!

The springs are on the inner mandibles. They come from one of the big car kits I believe.

As far as the other section, that’s the rear, bottom of the Falcon. Anything is possible. I’ve seen some small electronics L clips that are close to what’s there, but nothing 100%. I’m still leaning toward a kit part of some kind.

Hi John!

I pulled my Elf out and Part #15 doesn't look like it to me on the kit I have. Part #15 on my (E) sprue are both the same and both flat, like the one you pictured on the left side. The one on the right side in that scan does look like that funnel shape extends up though. Weird, being they're the exact same part on mine. Camera tricks? That would be a crazy camera trick! Same sprue position as the part I scanned and attached, as they're both right next to that control arm you can see sticking up in your scan.

The scans I attached are photos of the (E) sprue from my Tamiya Elf M.R.C. BS 1209 = 12009 (manufactured in 1973). There's another part on the (E) sprue (#12) that does have that funnel shape like the Falcon part, but it's lot taller than the Falcon part. You'd have to cut it down to match. It is hollow inside, so if it was cut there would be a hole in the middle which looks filled on the Falcon shot. Paint closed the hole, putty? I don't know, what are your thoughts? What's the control arm, or whatever part it is, that the part were looking for sits on top of on the Falcon? I can check my #12 up against that part if I have that kit here too to check scale. Although #12 might be too wide in the center compared to the original..

Jason

Click to expand...

Well ya see....twas about to try and flatten it out after cutting and sanding it  purdy like. Tried the Mrs. Revlon 8K HD hair dehumidifier and it just didn't get er done. So I said to myself, "Hey dummy, why not try a heat gun?" (I have obvious self loathing issues) So that' what we did.

Now before trying the old gentle cycle with the heat gun, I decided first to iron out some rumples in my cuttin' mat. Now after hitting it a few minutes with the temperature of the sun, and settin' some of them there heavy learning books on it, I had a nice flat mat.

HOWEVER....Ya see, I forgot, on an account I's ignorant, that she was still set on the surface of the sun settin'. So when I blasted that there tank hull, the itty bity piece right by the hatch....well it just done curled up like a one of them there Cuban ceegars.

I will take a stab at this.

 It certainly appears to be a Centurion tread from the multiple references I have. It appears to be mounted with its thin hinge forward. There is a vertical tab that seems to fit perfectly in between the slot in the bottom of blade actuator pistons joint. Thisself aligns things. Anyway,  that is what I have done for my 13 flaps on the rear engine deck.

Post #1492  (forum post #5415763)
Author    : Bjorn
Date      : Mar 24, 2023 at 3:56 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Also, is this the guy that was there before they drilled the handle holes?

View attachment 1682682View attachment 1682683

Click to expand...

The holes were there initially when they were building the core structure and before detailing. Makes it somewhat difficult to reproduce that way and get it to align properly.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/falcon14-jpg.1683223/]

Going off Nicks original ID thread, the Elefant piece is simply a plug to cover the holes and screws. Much like the Ferrari engine plates at the front of the mandibles, and the Semovente guns on the upper gun deck.

It stands to reason that all of these holes were plugged / covered early on in 76.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
