jcoffman99 said:

			Okay. 1.75 inches wide. I’m using .125 so when I add the top and bottom, it will be 2”. 16 7/8 long. 6mm at the back and 2mm between the Chevy and Pontoon. Thoughts?

View attachment 1684444

Click to expand...

Like it.  Looks good.  Hips and greeblies don't lie.

When I made my "mock-up" version, I was only thinking about the one key surface area, the sidewall itself.  When you actually build it, you have to think about the full "wrap-around" effect of the various chassis rails that go above and below, and how they sit on the upper and lower edge "lip" of this structure.  All that is to say is that you shouldn't glue anything down yet until you have these chassis rails affixed, and from that point, sequentially, you'll be able to more perfectly "center" the 57 Chevy and Pontoon Bridge pieces without having them sitting "too high" or "too low" in the overall siderail.  My first one was sitting noticeably low and looked wrong even though it had the right parts.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

No, it's certain, it's a 100% match, it's just that it's been trimmed off so much that just the top section is showing.  Look at the 8rad part #B30 on the actual kit, and you'll see it's the exact same shape you're describing with your line drawing and with your illustrations.  The track section to the right of it is also there, but it's "complete" in mine and only the very top edge of it in the PG Falcon sidewall pic, b/c that's how much (90%-ish) of those greeblies were shaved off of the bottom section of Matilda plate.  If you're noticing anything, it's probably that I placed my part #B30 "too high" on the plate so as to try to lose "as little as possible" of it when actually placing it on my build.

Post #1527  (forum post #5417049)

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman,

If you wonder why many of the Falcon Builders don't provide more details and responses, it's directly related to your attitude above to both StudioKitBash, JunkPilot and others.   You question many folks who give you the right answers, you knee jerk respond 'it's not it', then you find out after all their input was spot on because you didn't invest the time to actually find your own answers.   If you spent more time doing the research and investigating tips, you might've received more inputs.  As Tsophika mentioned, it is the correct part, you just didn't take the time to work out the trimming/orientation. Just because StudioKitBash may have gotten the part orientation wrong, you didn't look close enough to discover it is the part.

Spend some more time looking at references and working things out before judging the help from others.

If you would've just looked at the build threads/construction history that generous guys like Stu have provided out there, my own construction, and many others - you'd get a lot of answers AND you'd see parts just like the 8Rad right there on our builds. Yet you seem to refuse to leverage the work we have done.

Your attitude is also reflected in the title and content of this thread by saying "My....".   It's not 'your' reference thread, it's a collation of many thousands of hours of research and dollars by others - and rather than creating a thread that is 'Everyone's', you make it sound like 'yours' and there are perceptions that you have created all these maps - even though you give vague thanks to the previous reference makers.

To all the other RPF members, respectfully, I apologize for MY 'attitude', I had to vent here because it really gets frustrating to see the lack of initiative from jcoffman, and his near daily asks to  just 'tell me what this or that is'.   It's somewhat exhausting and frustrating for those of us that did a lot of the measuring, investing, etc... before we reached out to just tell folks to give them the info.

-Sean

Post #1529  (forum post #5417085)

Reactions : Like, Haha — joberg, swgeek, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			But the top section of the 8Rad part is flat. The Bandai slopes out. It looks like a slope on the actual model to me too.
		

Click to expand...

Yes, I noticed this too -- which means either:

a.) my #B30 should have been trimmed "down" from the base so that the part that meets the sidewall stars with the "sloped out" section instead of the flat section of this greeblie, or 

b.) that the Bandai PG kit part shows just how much the visual/perceptual slope of this part "looks sloped out" when in fact the trimming of the Matilda plate was done at roughly the same angle as the dome curvature "sloped in" and thus causes a "flat section" to appear "sloped out" if you follow what I'm saying.

But I'm leaning towards a.) as the correct interpretation, based on close examination and others feedback, which is, as always, appreciated.  I'm constantly amazed by how many "angles" of different reference photographs you need to really orient yourself to build this thing accurately, and when I make a mistake, I either make that same section over and over again (currently on my fourth Sheridan hull build!), or else I "own" my mistake as creative/artistic liberty and start quoting Han Solo all over again, "I've made a lot of special modifications to her" to acknowledge that yes, there is a difference between the original and mine, but it's a difference with which I'm happy to live.

But either way you interpret it, there is not any doubt that the greeblie ID on this area is 8rad #B30 or #B35

And hey, don't take it too hard -- at least no one on this particular comment thread is saying yours is "too small"!!!

; )

Sean,

I'm not sure exactly how to respond, but I'll try my best.

First off I've had a ton of help from lots of folks and am eternally grateful. Some has come publicly, while some has come via pm for fear of "reprisals". I think that is a sad statement on the hobby that folks feel like they can't talk in an open forum.

My "knee jerk" reactions are more of me thinking out loud. I can see how it could be interpreted that way, but after our virtual meet, I'm pretty sure those who were there know that's not my intention. 

Regarding the part in question, I've looked at a ton of photos from different angles and just didn't see it. I think I have it figured out now, but I do struggle with spatial relations. (Which is great for a project like this.) Also, I was sent two different pics of that plate by two different individuals so I thought I had a legit question. I always use the original source to confirm. As discoveries will always be made after a build.

As far as looking at others builds, I mentioned in our meet that I "try" not to use those pics as I do feel like it's cheating, or using the works of others. I usually only look if I'm trying to confirm something. I did look at your build in this case, but coupled with the two pics I was sent, I thought I had a legitimate question.

Spending time looking at reference is something I've done. Like every day, for hours, for over a year. My job allows a lot of free time and I spend most of the day either on SSModelers, looking at the available pics on the web, or those that have been shared with me. I can tell you I'm putting in the work. The problem is that a lot of the scans on ssmodeler were done over a decade ago and digital photography has improved a lot since them. A lot of the scans are so dark that details are hard to make out. That's one of the reasons I started my site.

On the title of the thread, you're absolutely right on that one. That title was created over a year ago by a guy who had noooooooo idea what he was getting into. I will change it to reflect the group effort, Thanks for bringing that to my attention.

I'm not sure why this couldn't have been  pm, but it seems like this whole thread really bothers you. If you're upset with what I'm doing, which you totally have the right to be, then maybe just don't come in the thread, or reach out via pm. 

Thanks,

John

Mate, fortunately I can see it both ways.

We can all be a bit dense at times, I certainly have my moments. Starting out here only a few years ago I was also super keen to blaze away.

Albeit, not in plain sight but generaly there never the less. You just have to do some digging as Sean rightfully suggested.

It takes a while to realise that all this work has already been done a few times over by the good folk that have gone before us. Sean being one of them.

I realise its also a bit nuts having to work it out yourself, its just how it is with these things sometimes. After all, that is the path to the light side.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So today I had the pleasure of talking to Alan on the phone. Such a nice and generous man. 

He doesn't do much online stuff, and stays out of the cloud so there's no real way for him to get me all of his photos. We can ask for specific areas, but I feel like that would become a nuisance quickly. He did say that if I came across any of his from other sources/people, he was fine with us using them.

So that being said, I'd be more than happy to host them on my drive. If you have some of them and would like to contribute, be it publicly or privately, please reach out via pm and we can hopefully find a was to make them available to everyone.

Thanks Alan!!!

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So I did a parts layout to try and find the waistband height.  Based on this section I got 2 5/8ths. Thoughts?

[IMAGE: 1329291-e7e6228844d2f25d28f05474ceb30e6b.jpg]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1556  (forum post #5418705)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Apr 2, 2023 at 8:38 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			So I did a parts layout to try and find the waistband height.  Based on this section I got 2 5/8ths. Thoughts?

View attachment 1686778

Click to expand...

My thoughts on this are simply that the FalGal plate you make is either going to be a "fixed" height or else a "custom" height because no two Falcons are the same sidewall height.  So I made mine "fat" with the hopes of squeezing everything into the sidewall without having to "trim" it like the ILM guys did, which to my aesthetic preference looks awful, but in all likelihood I'll just end up with whatever my sidewall height actually comes to, and then simply lay in my elements not in "plate" form but in originals like on the mandible sidewalls, so it looks inherently designed/built intentionally that way, rather than sloppy and rushed like a movie prop.  But if you want exact replica accuracy, warts and all, then 2-5/8ths sounds like a good starting point from which to start cutting down.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Thoughts?

Post #1668  (forum post #5424999)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : Apr 21, 2023 at 5:41 AM
Reactions : Like, Wow — manugu, joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This is all I could come up with so far, and none of them are right. Im guessing these are ladders in the kits?

In the Nichimo Ise kit: Part #48 (back side of sprue) from the Ise only has 6 “rungs”.

Whereas the part on the Falcon radar base looks like it has 15 rungs on the top two pieces, and 17 rungs on the bottom two pieces. Double check me please! Lost count a couple times. Starts getting blurry every time when your count starts getting to the end going top to bottom. LOL!

I checked quite a few kits that I have here and one of them, which is not a donor to this (as far as I know), is the Nichimo 1:200 Shiranu. Part # 22 & 23 come darned close at 14 rungs, but they’re too tall. Then that kit has a couple other parts #’s 73, 74, and 75 which are similar, but don’t have enough “rungs.”

The 1/500 Ise I have is the 1980 #U-5009. Maybe we need to find an older one? The 1/200 Shiranu is a 1976 #U-2014, not that it makes a difference. And as far as I can see, I don’t see a 1/200 Ise anywhere on the intranets. It’s probably right in front of my face here somewhere… as usual. Haha! Or it’s an older version of the Ise, which I don’t have on hand.

But as far as I can see with the actual Falcon parts we’re looking for, all those parts are shorter than the HP 0/400 piece. And the closest ones I show here are taller than the 0/400 piece.

Ise part # 48, back side on sprue. The part has a bend in it:

[IMAGE: 1335945-bbba5f340a5845b5288a22f28f26f0ca.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1335946-21a3b14c3ccf4fb32a5e346221836dee.jpg]

And the parts I see in the Shiranu kit: especially #22 and 23, but they are taller than the HP 0/400 piece and don’t have enough “rungs” on them. Plus 73,74, and 75 in the kit, but none of these are right either.

[IMAGE: 1335947-cf333d3016438eda18a3e9bb219a7127.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1335948-7640a8f803daa863f15c1dd94d0fc07b.jpg]

Anyways, it’s all about the effort! Haha! Too much effort I say for these darned little things! LOL

Post #1676  (forum post #5425901)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Apr 23, 2023 at 1:41 PM
Reactions : Like — Searun, joberg, JSchneider
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I think I found the missing radar dish leg pieces:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/screenshot-2023-04-22-092409-jpg.1693776/]

These four guys must come from the original vintage Nichimo 1/500 Ise ship, and not the new/reboxed one that is also boxed as the Hyuga:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1682282065571-jpeg.1694347/]

You want the kit on bottom, not the one on top.

The instructions for the vintage kit look like this:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1682281997168-jpeg.1694346/]

and if you look closely, you'll notice that in a separate bag you get a different set of microgreeblies (on separate black sprue, not grey) than what you get in the modern boxings:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1682282172858-jpeg.1694348/]

I'm fairly confident that the 4 pieces you're looking for are "2 tall" and "2 short" pieces from Parts #122.

And of course, I say "fairly confident" because my kit came with everything EXCEPT these pieces, so I don't have them to photograph and compare and count 17 ladder rungs on, but I know they are in the black sprue separate baggie by comparison to the modern kit, and because part #50 was the mystery greeblie that evaded me for years on the Y-Wing wing subassembly.  And they look to be around the right size, in comparison with part #50 which is only about 4.5mm tall.  

Anyway, that's my theory.  Anyone who's built one want to confirm or deny?
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #1732  (forum post #5430163)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : May 5, 2023 at 9:06 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Studio Kitbash said:

			I think I found the missing radar dish leg pieces:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/screenshot-2023-04-22-092409-jpg.1693776/]

These four guys must come from the original vintage Nichimo 1/500 Ise ship, and not the new/reboxed one that is also boxed as the Hyuga:

View attachment 1694347

You want the kit on bottom, not the one on top.

The instructions for the vintage kit look like this:

View attachment 1694346

and if you look closely, you'll notice that in a separate bag you get a different set of microgreeblies (on separate black sprue, not grey) than what you get in the modern boxings:

View attachment 1694348

I'm fairly confident that the 4 pieces you're looking for are "2 tall" and "2 short" pieces from Parts #122.

And of course, I say "fairly confident" because my kit came with everything EXCEPT these pieces, so I don't have them to photograph and compare and count 17 ladder rungs on, but I know they are in the black sprue separate baggie by comparison to the modern kit, and because part #50 was the mystery greeblie that evaded me for years on the Y-Wing wing subassembly.  And they look to be around the right size, in comparison with part #50 which is only about 4.5mm tall.

Anyway, that's my theory.  Anyone who's built one want to confirm or deny?
		

Click to expand...

Hey Read!

Next time you're by your kits and the "Hyuga" could you snap a pic of these parts I circled in red? The 1980 ISE kit is also different in this area. I only have what looks to be #17 in your kit (Part #51 in my ISE kit) (x3). This kit does not have #6, or #8. I'm still working on radar trying to find these, and curious to know what these parts look like in your kit. Although the parts manual doesn't exactly have this shape, I figured it's worth a shot! If these aren't it, could take a look in your kit and see if you see the parts under the dish that I circled in the radar pic?

Thank you in advance!

So #4 is the Kettenkrad and while looking at the Kettenkrad I found this piece that has fallen off, so I've update this map.

jcoffman99 said:

View attachment 1698182

Click to expand...

Hey John... I just pulled Kettenkrad again... I have Esci and Hasegawa, both have same part numbers so I'm assuming yours are the same too, or are you going off a scan?

I thought when we were looking at that rear port sidewall we were thinking of possibly using at least one of either Kettenkrad part #154, or #155 for the piece that attaches to the underdome. As for the other part that sits on the sidewall I still don't know what that could be and haven;t found anything yet. I did look for the part you mentioned earlier, which is #109 and I found it in one of the kits. I also found one more similar piece looking through the kit again. Funny how you can't see the forest for the trees sometimes, isn't it? That's my new favorite saying this week! Haha! I think I only saw 154 and 155 last time I looked, now there's 4 parts all of a sudden in there that look the part.

Out of all of them I would still guess that at least one of those two (154 or 155) is used there to attach under the dome, but I still don't see any of them working on the sidewall part of it. 109 (is curved on the side a bit. The part you have listed.), 119 (is too much work), or 154-155 (I think maybe the long one, 155)? There are Kettenkrad parts around there, so it's not a giant leap. I would still like to know what was glued up to the sidewall though... Sometimes things jump right out at you, sometimes they don't. What do you think about it?

Still working on the back of radar and those few pieces yet. Haven't found them yet (thought I did a couple times! Haha!), and that one Hummel piece under the A-Frame... I don't think that's Hummel, or can't find it (or them) in there. Still looking. Ugh!

BTW, it's Saturday, raining, and I'm three years into this thing with six years total if you count the Y-Wing research and build (which gave me the idea that I could build a Falcon), and I've gotta say, when my wife asks me, "What are you doing today?" and I reply, "Looking for needles in haystacks with a handful of equally obsessed nerds" she just chuckles quietly and goes and makes some tea.  But I LOVE the fact that you guys are just as hyper-focused on finding all the tiny/fiddly/missing/hiding/mystery parts as I am -- easily the most fun jigsaw puzzle I've ever worked on.

So thanks:  this is a great group of weirdoes we've assembled here, and I'm grateful to be a part of it.

Back to one of the other pieces… and I should add that there have been those that came before us that deserve ALL the credit for pointing us in the right direction to a lot of these parts! If it weren't for those that came before us, I don't think we'd be as far along as we are in our journeys! So a BIG THANK YOU from me to all those that blazed this trail long before us. I had an idea that this part may come from this specific kit due to all the hard work done in the past! Although it still took me a while to figure out what part we might have to use from this kit before I figured it out. A "Have to double check everything and make sure before your sure" kind of thing. Haha! Just want to put that disclaimer here, and say thank you from me!

How about Airfix 0/400 # 127 & 128 for the parts glued to the side of Stug/Saturn? Cut out 4 of them? What say you all?

Post #1774  (forum post #5431780)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : May 10, 2023 at 2:33 PM
Reactions : Like — greeble_gremlin, Bjorn, manugu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Hey everyone, I could use your opinions before I can't turn back.... Haha!

Found a pic where you can definitely see that the Saturn ring is stacked on top of the stug ring. The stug color is plainly visible, and you can barely make out the joint between the two parts in in this pic. Very hard to see this in all the reference photos, and this is the best shot I've seen in my pics that shows this distinctly.

[IMAGE: 1342575-75fe29bfc1ea2b65a624502fd9e650d4.jpg]

Then what do you think about this? Awful lot of work for these two parts here!

[IMAGE: 1342578-4cc2f424ed2e52a5c4ff46c0b815080e.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342579-637fae8d71a0dc1791f57c1f9ffca552.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342580-b9b9ec61076cfb727ad557be747edeac.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342581-d33cf5d65897f0e4e2c4c51c87ea75af.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342582-30a9c01450ccfb2a58bee6672931b819.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342583-79f51adb36ea81cef3c29e1225d1d579.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342584-2d8a2a9fe33c0cf013e06982c8913c77.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342585-4fe86a18275a18c56266884b20fd5cc5.jpg]

Then as for total height: I think it’s shorter than this in the reference, but might try double the height of the Stug hinge first so there’s room for error. Here is the Saturn ring cut (the ring itself, NOT including the Stug ring height) at 0.36” tall.

[IMAGE: 1342587-2cd2f790fc9d43b94985968e1ac38961.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342588-b16bb1e82ecb8d1a8f192cfe593343cb.jpg]

But in the reference of Falcon it doesn’t look like you could stack 2 of those Stug hinges within the Saturn rings height, if you get what I’m saying. So by laying two hinges tall I made a mark at half the height of the second hinge. So what it comes down to is cutting it at 0.3” tall. I think this might be the correct height.

[IMAGE: 1342589-142f24e95581dafff47a5ec2ce1aa8d5.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342590-b6181dbc2f16929db13e0db1c3a9e15e.jpg]

Just wanted your opinion’s before I cut this. Seems like a lot of work for these two parts compared to the rest of the radar so far! Sheesh! Haha!

Thanks everyone!

P.S. Thanks to “The Guy” who provided me with this unbelievable casting of the Stug ring so I don’t have to sacrifice the real thing while doing this! You know who you are. But if I screw this one up, “I’ll be back!” Haha!

That looks like a pretty good approach to figure out the Saturn ring height.  Has it ever been questioned/challenged if it's a Saturn V ring or not?  The only reason I ask is because at that height it could be a tank wheel or rim, and it seems "odd" to me that they'd find a ring of the right OD but not the right height and need to cut it down, but then again a lot of things are odd on this bird.

Post #1780  (forum post #5432075)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : May 11, 2023 at 2:21 PM
Reactions : Like, Love — Dimitri-Ajith, greeble_gremlin, Bjorn
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Okay… here it is. Looks like everything works out. You need to cut the Stug ring down to right around 1.78” in diameter to match the Saturn ring OD. So far in fact, there’s not much left of the Stug ring. So be careful. I had more left on the casting, than the actual part. It gets VERY thin there!

Then you have the cut the Saturn ring height down to your preference. I went with about 0.30” tall in these photos. I spread the Stug hinges out a little bit to better hold the wire. I used plastruct butyrate coated wire. I can’t find the invoice, but it is the smallest size they have which comes in black or gray.

I have to tell you I was concerned when I opened the Saturn box and started looking at the part. I didn’t think the side angle was sloped enough. But once it’s together it looks alright.

[IMAGE: 1342819-eff6fb25c8339fcf61d25787a1352ac8.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342820-d039a9b014d019e61db0654868bdaedb.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342821-fea11ba3f211bcf6480101952d6d6303.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342823-cd21c4d99b4325fee79f72919af3ce2e.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342827-f251652263bc5ef6bfa05df81b9781ca.jpg]

And when looking at Stug ring from behind you can tell they used 8 ISE Hyuga guns on the outside edge as the supports to glued to the dish, but you can also see that there is enough room for some extra center ISE guns towards the center of ring. So I would assume those inside supports we saw in the photos are also from ISE/Hyuga too.

And probably best to take a piece of sandpaper and lay on top of dish and sand the ISE guns down so they match the profile of the dish. So they all make contact and fit perfectly on there when it gets glued. I added 4 more guns for inside support. Just my two cents.

[IMAGE: 1342824-b97f70d6e5058088c71a0a1b92d8873f.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342837-9b6df0c15ade9c11b5e6b1e39726828c.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342828-5b258cd92036f1446a747808d21ff9c1.jpg]

[IMAGE: 1342836-694aac824513c067a9843d2cb243d6eb.jpg]

 

Attachments

[IMAGE: 1342826-54f4f345efb69e8721181ff9ee6d9d37.jpg]

C1AB7A90-1B8F-4BAA-ACA4-F8D3B807F12A.jpeg

Post #1783  (forum post #5432103)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : May 11, 2023 at 4:34 PM
Reactions : Haha, Like — jcoffman99, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Thanks for letting me post about this John! I appreciate it. I know that it's an "Open Source" thread now... 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]
, but don't mean to hijack anything. LOL That Saturn part just looked off to me when I opened the kit up and for about 20 minutes the thought crossed my mind "That can't be it...?". I just wanted to run it by some of our fellow cohorts to make sure I wasn't losin' it that the parts height got cut down that far. Haha! Threw me off a little and made me start questioning things a bit.  Maybe even another contributing factor was that I have been staring at the map so long lately looking for all the little stuff my brain even thought for a minute at first that the "Saturn" parts side might be curved since the colored in version does look curved to me for some reason in the map, until you pull up the reference photos. WOW! "Question EVERYTHING. Trust No One." que X-Files theme song... LOL

This would be helpful info for any future weirdos and nutjobs that want to build this thing as well I figured. Now back to our regularly scheduled programming... Haha!

Tip 'o the Day: Make sure before you start laying things out, know damn well that YOU ARE going to get the bug to just go ahead and glue it all up while you're doing it. Why do it twice if you don't have to? LOL Always, always, always make sure you have toothpicks in the house... and when you run out of micro glue applicator tips, make sure you order them immediately otherwise you'll forget and not have them when you need them! You'll end up gluing your fine point tweezers and some other improvised mini-tools together trying to use them as a toothpick, and you'll end up making a mess of everything anyways on top of it. I thought I had built up more patience than that by this point. Haha!

Just came across this little tidbit of information…

Apparently the ILM sound team went to the Osh Kosh air show (in Wisconsin) in 1976’ish to record sound for A New Hope. They recorded some P-51’s as sound for the Falcon. The info that caught me most though is that ILM sent the EAA (Experimental Aircraft Association) a model of the Falcon after the movie came out which they still have, and I would assume is still on display at EAA’s museum up there. They assume was used during filming, but aren’t sure what scenes it may have been used in. 

When I first saw the story I got my hopes up it was another big model of the Falcon that I could go visit and get some photos of for research for my build, but it seems to be a much smaller model than the 5 footer. I’m only about 2 1/2 hours from Osh Kosh and would totally drive up to see it. Even if it is a small model, and the yearly air show is coming up here soon, so maybe I’ll go up there this year and check it out.

Does anyone have any info on which model it is that they have, and if it was used during filming ANH at all? Just curious.

Here’s a link to the story which has a local news broadcast with video of the model itself. 

Star Wars and EAA: creating Academy Award magic
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Star Wars and EAA: creating Academy Award magic

Click to expand...

I knew the Falcon sound was a P-51 and there was only one Falcon was made for Star Wars, being the 5ft Falcon. This gifted Falcon was NOT used in the original film and is actually the MPC kit that came out in 1978 AFTER the film's release. If anything it COULD have been used in The Empire Strikes Back but even then that is doubtful since three other smaller Falcons were build for Empire, the 32in, 8in and 2in versions.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1816  (forum post #5440619)
Author    : JSchneider

This gifted Falcon was NOT used in the original film and is actually the MPC kit that came out in 1978 AFTER the film's release. If anything it COULD have been used in The Empire Strikes Back but even then that is doubtful since three other smaller Falcons were build for Empire, the 32in, 8in and 2in versions.
		

Click to expand...

Thanks for that. That's what I had figured only having ever heard about the five footer being the only actual filming prop for the first movie, and them barely having enough time to build the model for shooting after changing things up at the last minute. After reading into it a little bit (barely thus far), it seemed that EAA may have received the model from ILM before MPC had released their version of it in 1979 (or so I thought), so I was wondering what it may have been. Maybe ILM did receive a kit from MPC before releasing it, and they painted one up for EAA. I had never heard about this before, and a bit of local lore I guess. I've been up to the air show quite a few times in my lifetime and had never seen this, that I remember anyway.

While we're on the subject, does anyone know if the 5 footer is out on display anywhere right now? Seems to be that it's locked up in the archives at the moment as I haven't seen anything lately about it being on tour somewhere, but was hoping there's some info out there I haven't seen yet. Have separate vacations with unplanned locations as of yet coming up with my daughter for getting her BA, and then the wife. Was thinking if I could find it somewhere, I could subliminally mention a vacay location and sneak a trip in to see it during either one, without mentioning it! Shhh... LOL

There are a couple partially built kits listed locally that would make great adds to my Falcon stash that I've been holding off on because of this issue.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1829  (forum post #5451754)
Author    : Rahn420
Date      : Jul 18, 2023 at 10:10 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Necronaut
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Necronaut said:

			Does the freezer trick work for model glue (ie. Testors tube cement, Tamiya Extra Thin) on these kits, or is it just CA glue that can cleanly 'snap' apart after being in the freezer?  There are a couple partially built kits listed locally that would make great adds to my Falcon stash that I've been holding off on because of this issue.
		

Click to expand...

Don't know if this helps...

When I was a kid (like 50 years ago) I had the Aurora Godzilla model with the glow-in-the-dark parts.

It was glued with Testors red tube glue (was there anything else, back then?).

I  had put the head near a light bulb to 'charge' it up.

When I picked up the model, I found that the heat from the bulb softened the glue to where I was able to pull the head off.

I could also stick the head back on, and when cool, it was glued solid again.

Upon this discovery, Godzilla became my 'stash' keeper for some time.

Of course, Godzilla's head was a pretty beefy part. Not sure how the heat would work on smaller, more delicate parts.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1830  (forum post #5451770)
Author    : swgeek
Date      : Jul 18, 2023 at 11:05 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Necronaut
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Also, I've had pretty good luck using Easy-off (yes the oven cleaner) to get paint off of plastic. I'd try it on a piece you don't care about just to be sure. They're always changing the make up of products.

So a little trick that I find helps...If your molds are open face molds that are flat on the open side, you can sand the plastic flat with the back of the mold and eliminate most of the flashing.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So three pieces of 3/4” plywood plus my top and bottom works out to exactly 2 5/8” for the patty thickness. What do you think?

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/006ab80c-db89-485b-a671-d6f4c420de80-jpeg.1730623/]

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/37267bc6-153b-4738-a3ff-d1cc1d69b563-jpeg.1730624/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1873  (forum post #5461361)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Aug 20, 2023 at 8:50 AM
Reactions : Like — JSchneider, joberg, bwayne64
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			So three pieces of 3/4” plywood plus my top and bottom works out to exactly 2 5/8” for the patty thickness. What do you think?

View attachment 1730623View attachment 1730624

Click to expand...

Looks pretty spot on to my eye.  Make sure you like your OD first before making any permanent commitments, because once you nail the height you still have to spend anxious hours fretting about too much or too little or just enough lateral wall real estate to place everything, and while you will indubitably end up fudging here or there by spreading out or compressing in some pieces, you for sure want to have a nice "rough layout" that you're happy with before you lay down any epoxy or CA on the sidewalls.  This is the approach I'm taking, at least, as I try to get to this next hurdle in the coming year.  It's the part where you realize that in order to really successfully reverse-engineer a Falcon you have to build two Falcons -- one to screw it up completely and one to get it right by learning from the first one.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So here's what I came up with today for the mandibles.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1692912484783-png.1731879/]

Post #1919  (forum post #5464331)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Aug 29, 2023 at 6:09 AM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Okay I need to rethink my armature. 

With only a 2 5/8 patty, a 2” pipe isn’t going to work. There’s not enough “meat” to mount it. Also, it’s way too heavy. 

Anyone try aluminum pipe? Is it strong enough? Have any other suggestions? 

Thanks,

John
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1920  (forum post #5464368)
Author    : bwayne64
Date      : Aug 29, 2023 at 8:18 AM
Reactions : Like — JSchneider
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Okay I need to rethink my armature.

With only a 2 5/8 patty, a 2” pipe isn’t going to work. There’s not enough “meat” to mount it. Also, it’s way too heavy.

Anyone try aluminum pipe? Is it strong enough? Have any other suggestions?

Thanks,

John
		

Click to expand...

People are using 2 inch aluminum pipe on their Studio Scale  Galacticas, and that is a heavy beast. Its 1.90 " OD,  fairly light and very strong. It's used as hand railings. They also sell various connectors and fittings as well.

Here's a catalog page from Julius Blum who makes the railing system. They also sell a 1.6 inch OD version. It's a little ways down the page. Hope this helps. I bought mine locally from a metal supply co. You might save on cost and shipping if you have a metal supply near you.

						Architectural Stock Components Catalog - Julius Blum & Co. Inc.
					

Explore the comprehensive Architectural Stock Components Catalog by Julius Blum & Co. Inc. Find premium quality components for your architectural projects.

[IMAGE: favicon-32x32.png]

					www.juliusblum.com

Post #1922  (forum post #5464374)
Author    : bwayne64

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Thank you so much. That’s a big help.
		

Click to expand...

Your welcome ; ) Another idea is to use

 steel conduit for canopies. You can get various sizes. It's fairly strong when it's a short section, which is what you'll have in the Falcon. You'll need to support it with bulkheads. As long as your mounting rod is strong enough it should be more than adequate. It's steel so heavier than the aluminum tubing, buts it's fairly thin walled, so not too heavy.

Post #1925  (forum post #5464851)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : Aug 30, 2023 at 1:15 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Okay I need to rethink my armature.

With only a 2 5/8 patty, a 2” pipe isn’t going to work. There’s not enough “meat” to mount it. Also, it’s way too heavy.

Anyone try aluminum pipe? Is it strong enough? Have any other suggestions?

Thanks,

John
		

Click to expand...

I've been thinking the whole time about using 2" aluminum square tubing bent into the round to the outer perimeter of the waistband (minus whatever thickness I use for my gluing surface for the waist (maybe .125 styrene wrapped around the outside perimeter?), with a 1.60" main cross pipe, and 1" (maybe 3/4"?) mandible support tubes welded to the main pipe and come through the front of the 2" square tubing. It would look similar to what ILM did with the wooden armature and tubing, but all aluminum and lighter.

I have a steel shop nearby that says they can get a perfect circle with the 2" square tubing on their bender, but I'd almost have to cut it in half to weld the main pipe through the center. And i'd have to account for the DR notch-outs. I'm worried things would get out of round and out of square having to cut it in half to fit that pipe through that comes out through the docking rings. I was thinking that would end up being lighter than using the wood and a bit more robust. One other thing that worried me is adhering my styrene around the outer edge for the waistband being such dissimilar materials. And I'm still hung up on how I want to attach to the mount. Someone around here had the great idea of using an exhaust tubing expander to expand inside the main tube to hold steady on the mount. I'm a little worried though that using aluminum pipe instead of steel for the main pipe would end up bending the main tube, and there's also the issue of getting the exhaust expander mounted to the engine stand.

 I'm still running the scenario through my head after all these months and have committed to nothing yet for the armature. LOL!

How are you going to mount to the stand John? Weld a long tube onto the engine stand that slides inside the armature tube and drill some holes through both tubes to be able to throw a bolt in and lock it in place so it can't spin?

I “think” I’m going with 1.5 “ pipe. The pipe will go to a 2” pipe via a steel reducer. That 2” pipe with go into the engine stand with a hole drilled in it to lock it with a pin.

I’ll probably weld a vertical piece to mount it from the bottom. That’s how it will end up being displayed. 

Thoughts?
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #1928  (forum post #5465102)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : Aug 31, 2023 at 8:35 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			I “think” I’m going with 1.5 “ pipe. The pipe will go to a 2” pipe via a steel reducer. That 2” pipe with go into the engine stand with a hole drilled in it to lock it with a pin.

I’ll probably weld a vertical piece to mount it from the bottom. That’s how it will end up being displayed.

Thoughts?
		

Click to expand...

Trying to picture it... Where would the reducer be placed (inside the body, or outside?) And where would the hole be drilled (inside, or outside?) I'm guessing outside for both, right?

When I've been thinking about my options if I go this route... I don't want the main internal pipe extending past the docking rings obviously. If I end up using a tube that slides inside another tube I was thinking I'd want the larger diameter tube inside the body, and I'd want to drill the hole for the pin in the center so I could access under the turrets because the turrets will be removable. (DR bodies could be removable too to be able to drill the locking pin there as well, but then all the weight is to the side as well.) I also worry that if I use a pipe that slides into another pipe, the weight of the Falcon may eventually over time crack the pipe weld on the mount with all the weight being on that one small area. So that will have to be beefed up a bit. I wish I knew how much it will weigh when we're done... 80-100 pounds? And the biggest thing I would need to pay attention to is that the armature tubes ID and the mounting pipe OD are going to play nice together, that they fit inside each other without too much slop. I haven't even looked at buying an engine stand yet, so I'm not sure yet how my mounting plate will be set up yet.

Sounds like you're going 2" from the mount, to slide over 1.5" main structure pipe. Am I reading that right? Would they mate inside the DR body, or just outside of it? Can you fit 1 3/4" through your wooden armature and still leave some meat there, or would 1 3/4" still be too big? I haven't started looking at pipe yet, but they have thin walled and thick walled John which all have different OD's. We just have to find pipe that plays well together. Decisions, decisions. All that matters is that it works! LOL

I’m picturing your using a threaded reducer. So the reducer will be threaded onto the 2” mount pipe, and threaded onto the 1 1/2” pipe (inside the docking ring body?) And the 2” pipe will run through the mount and be pinned. The OD of that reducer will be rather large I’m guessing, so the hole cut into the docking ring face would have to be large too. Post #1932  (forum post #5465134)
Author    : JSchneider
Date      : Aug 31, 2023 at 10:11 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Dimitri-Ajith
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			The reducer will be outside the ship and only temporary. Since I'm going to mount the ship from the bottom, I really don't need access to the sides once it's finished. My stand has the arm for swinging.
		

Click to expand...

I was going to ask too… are you planning on aluminum armature pipe, and then steel mount pipe and reducer? If you thread the aluminum pipe you just have to be careful don’t cross thread it cause it’s so soft comparatively. And steel to aluminum corrodes pretty easily, so if it stays together for long periods of time they may seize together. It’ll be indoors, so might not be an issue but you can use anti-seize on the threads.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			Just playing around. I need to get thicker plating. What’s your go to thickness? 1mm?  This is some .3 I had around. It’s great for cutting with scissors. Lol. Need a little thicker.

View attachment 1736402

Click to expand...

A certain someone around here (you know who you are!) turned me on to this GodHand cutter. It works well, but rather expensive like every other GodHand. I love their stuff though. And replacement blades were a little tricky to find locally, but you can get 50 packs of them.

Godhand Amazing Cutter - Walmart, Amazon, model shop websites, etc. I think I paid around $130-$150 for them.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1693869886502-png.1736410/]

I have had a pair of Leatherman Raptor Shears for years for work. They work really well for cutting styrene, along with a whole bunch of other hard to cut items. EMT's and fireman usually have them for car accidents and rescues. They have come in really handy over the years cutting a wide variety of things and they're cheaper than the GodHands. You can break car windows with the rescue shears too among a bunch of other stuff. Multipurpose. They fold up too and have a little belt case to store them in. I have gotten mine on Amazon and I think they were $80-$100. These are nice if you have long cuts to follow and just use them like scissors. Think these were around $80-$90 last pair I bought.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1693870114231-png.1736412/]

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1693870617760-png.1736418/]

As for the armor, I haven't gotten that far yet, but I was going to start with 0.30 and see how that looked. I think I have .030 written down for the mandibles and a mix of armor for the dome, but .040" to .060" mostly if I recall correctly, with thinner layers lain underneath where the big gash damage in the lower hull is.  But .030 looks too thin on the mandible, huh? I think .040 = 1mm, yeah?

Click to expand...

Here’s a screenshot of the instructions from the GodHands with what Olfa blades to get. Just for reference so you can get the right replacement blades. Thank goodness for Google translate!!! Haha!

There’s a plastic backer plate that needs to be replaced occasionally too that the blade comes down on when cutting. It’s just a 3mm square piece of Plastruct styrene (or ABS would work too I suppose) that fits in there. I’d recommend you get some in case that gets screwed up too. Otherwise the cutter won’t work right if the plastic gets damaged too.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/img_5818-jpeg.1736423/]

THis is the front side. I didn't get any charing on this because I've added an air assist. Cuts down greatly on the charing. Honestly as clean as this cut is, I don't think I'll need to sand. It looks rougher in the pic because I moved it a few times while trying different settings. However, once I got those dialed in, it cut razor sharp. I have a hood that sucks out all the fumes, so I should be safe...cough...cough...wheeze...

Yea, I never cut a kit part unless it's cheap and easily found. Some of these guys make me cringe, how easily they can cut up a $ 500 kit, lol. More power to them if they have the cash. I'm way too tight for that. My grandfather would come back and poltergeist me, if I did such a thing,

; ) It is easier and cheaper to use original parts, but only the non rare ones. I just bought 9 more Flakvierling Ah 52s, cause they were super cheap. And molding some of those parts, would make me pull out hair, lol.

Post #2120  (forum post #5480283)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Oct 17, 2023 at 10:09 AM
Reactions : Like — JSchneider, manugu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

jcoffman99 said:

			I'm doing a major cleanup of my kit list document and have the following kits on it, that I haven't ID'd parts for yet. Do I need these kits?

Airfix- Panzer IV

Bandai - 88mm Flak

Fujimi- Elefant

Tamiya- 20mm Gun, SD 222, SD 223, Semovente

Thanks!
		

Click to expand...

Airfix 1/72 Panzer IV, track link sidewall plates L/R needed for the ORIGINAL upper gun turret subassembly.

Fujimi 1/76 Elefant is a donor to the X-Wing and Y-Wing, but not the Falcon as far as I know

Tamiya 1/35 20mm Flakvierling - only used on ESB version of Falcon

Tamiya 1/35 SD 222 - Not used

Tamiya 1/35 SD 223 - Not used
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Well....and then there were none. This is the last one. I know there are a few pieces here and there to figure out, but as far as maps go, this is it.

I cannot begin to thank everyone for all of the help over the last almost two years. The amount of time and resources that were shared was amazing, and I'm truly grateful to you all.  I'm also thankful for the friendships I've made. You are all awesome people and talented builders.

I also would like to throw out that if there is anything you would like mapped, let me know. I really enjoy doing this. It's relaxing to me. Plus it keeps me busy. So if there's something out there the group would like to see done, let me know.

Here's the link to all the finished maps on my drive:

Finished Part Maps

Thanks again!

John

I'm in the middle of researching the top turret to be built as a standalone piece. Looking through this thread I can see numerous references to the changes between ANH and ESB versions, but the base photos for the part maps (I think) are predominantly ESB, and the issue is compounded by the differences between the top and bottom turrets.

Does anyone have a handle on the differences between the 4 versions ({ANH,ESB},{Top,Bottom})? For example the use of the Semovente parts vs the Panzer IV parts for the screw covers is clear to differentiate top and bottom, (hope you don't mind me using an excerpt from your part map John) but not whether these are the ANH or ESB turrets.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/screenshot-2023-10-20-at-15-40-18-png.1752795/]

The part circled below is one of the differences. It looks like an ammunition box and it's NOT a Sheridan part as identified in some part maps. However, if it's an ESB mod then I can skip it if I build the ANH variant (Although it's going to bug me continuously.)

Don't know if you know about this site. I use it all the time, to see what's been changed over the years. As far as this site there have been parts added in 2020. It hasn't been retooled as far as I know. The box and decals have changed but not the kit. I have the older kit. It's always good practice to get the vintage kit, if you can find and afford it, ; ) I don't know if they removed parts, so I didn't really answer the question, lol..

						Timeline for American LaFrance Ladder Chief, AMT 1204 (2020) - Timeline
					

The full history of 1204 AMT Ladder Chief in scale 1:25, quickly see all reboxings and new tools.

Click to expand...

Yeah, the Arado is annoyingly necessary:  that little piece (n.b. Part #33, NOT #34) not only tells you if you built your rear landing gear box at the right angle, but it also tells you why you can't parallelize the back of the landing gear box with the Shiden structural-wing piece that lays on the very end of it, which to me always looked like it was in need of "fixing".
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

It certainly appears that way. I have been using Tt gauge model railroad track on this Falcon section.

S gauge “American Flyer“ and HO gauge work in other Falcon areas with even some O gauge “Lionel” track in T-rail and I -beam locations if the correct styrene structural shape does not fit your needs.

Necronaut said:

			Merry Christmas, John!  A truly envious haul!

this just dropped on YT.  The first 20-odd seconds drops a bunch of 5’ drawings and blueprints on the walls of Lorne Peterson’s office.  Might be nice for us 5’ builders?

Click to expand...

That's a great video and it also confirms how ILM referred to the Falcon sizes as well. I recall reading somewhere that the Falcons were referred to as the "4ft" and "2ft" Falcons by ILM. In the video Adam talks about the "3ft" Falcon (referring to the 32in model) and moments later Lorne calls it the "2ft" Falcon, pretty much confirming the size was based on the size of the acrylic domes used for the main hull of both models.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2270  (forum post #5500593)
Author    : eagle1
Date      : Dec 28, 2023 at 3:04 AM
Reactions : Like — rbeach84, Junk Pilot, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Yes, that's correct in the ILM terminology for the sizing of the Falcon miniatures.

I was corrected recently by someone in the know, to how the Falcon miniatures were described, with what I & others call the 5 footer, is actually the 4 foot model & the 32" ESB Falcon miniature is the 2 foot model. 

All goes off the dish size, not length!. 

Stu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So one last comment/question on the lower gun platform and I'll hopefully leave it be 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

On the Bandai kit, there's a 'spikey-bit' just to the port of the actual gun mount. It's sort-of-kinda visible on the photo references of the filming model, but there are not a lot of good shots of the lower platform. Anyway, as near as I can tell, that part is the Fujimi M36 Jackson, part B15.

[edit] - Also this assumes Bandai got it right and this wasn't an ESB add on. I've found quite a lot of extra, missing, and incorrectly sized parts on that kit, so it could be just a Bandai kit thing.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1704301984513-png.1775460/]

Post #2287  (forum post #5508870)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Jan 28, 2024 at 5:42 PM
Reactions : Like — rbeach84, Necronaut, Dimitri-Ajith
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So I've been working on some file organization, picture taking, and behind the scenes stuff this weekend. 

I've been frustrated with the speed over on Scifikitbash.com and am in the process of moving hosts. This will take a view days as the transfer process is a pain. However, in doing this ,I've noticed just how big my kit scan image files are. 

I've been working to compress them so that they'll load faster without losing the details. Only now have I discovered the miracle of batch editing in Photoshop.

It will take a long time to do this, but I think it will be worth it and hope it helps folks. You will notice on my drive that I'm using the color green to denote which folders have been already compressed. Right now there is only one. 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

If you have any other ideas or suggestions, please feel free.

Thanks,

John

Post #2335  (forum post #5521769)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Mar 17, 2024 at 1:07 PM
Reactions : Like — Rick Deckard, joberg, manugu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Okay, so I may have solved my own mystery.

It looks like it's a cut down of the Tamiya Hanomag C28 sitting on top of the Hasegawa Sherman #17 Sitting on top of Hasegawa Sherman #5.

Sorry for the bad photos, apparently my iPhone doesn't do macro very well.

Anyway, if this is correct then I hope it helps someone. If it's not correct, please let me know. And maybe we should update the parts maps for the docking rings.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1710705943313-png.1800929/]

jcoffman99 said:

			Can someone refresh my brain please? Were the color Chronicles pictures after ANH filming? There is considerable difference around the sides of the engine in the color photos versus the black and white. Trying to nail down which is the ANH version.
		

Click to expand...

I don’t know the dates of the Chronicle images, just noticed there are also small differences between the color images. In some a top engine flap is missing for instance, in others it‘s there. The b/w have all kinds of configurations of the gun turrets, so most likely not taken at the same day either. Hard to put them into a consistent timeline. The good thing this shows is that a ‚perfect‘ Falcon replica is most likely impossible, so we can stop bothering 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

Edit: Whops, did not recognize that the turret images on page 68 are actually top and bottom, so the b/w images might actually come from the same day. (Just for the 2 other people out there who care about this)

Edit2: Thanks Read for the translation you posted 2 years ago which I should have read more carefully: 

“ black-and-white images are a valuable photographic record taken on October 30, 1978 by VFX Supervisor Dennis Murren“

So please ignore all my ignorant rumbling above 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]

If that's the case, I do not feel bad creating a replica. If it's ever discovered what that really is, and I've not started painting, I'll swap it out.

In the mean time, here's my stab at modelling it based on Craig's photos and measurements taken from my Sherman part. If anyone has measurements from X-Wing castings I can adjust my model accordingly.

The part in Craig's photos show sink marks on the top. I don't know if those were in the source part, or are the result of subsequent castings shown in the photos. For the purposes of the Falcon, it doesn't matter anyway because there's a Hasegawa Sherman Bogie going on top.

I've attached the STL if anyone wants to give it a go. (Tanker_96.stl)

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1712326127752-png.1807347/]

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Added the Nichimo Centurion to everything.

There is a tan version or a green version that comes in this same box.  

You will notice that part #61 is slightly different. In the tan version, it has lines running across it. In the green version it is flat.

The fan built Falgal panel pics that are floating around of just this area have the one with the lines. However, the actual Falcon seems to use the flat version.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/centurion-hatches-jpg.1809615/]

Yep, that's how I did mine and it turned out okay. It's a bit off, and I'm not sure why, the same parts should line up the same as the original. But I think it's pretty close, and when painted few will notice the slight errors.  

I had a bit of luck with the UBoat. I purchased one kit, used and open, and discovered that, while some of it had been started, it actually had 3 sprue B's in the box, along with other duplicate parts. So one purchase got me all the railings I needed.

Be aware that not all the uboat railings are the same length.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1713143764951-png.1810129/]

View attachment 1810129

Click to expand...

There's actually two types of railing in the U-boat kit!. Not all railings are alike & both types were utilized in a certain pattern. It's easy to miss these with a few glances, but once you know & see it, you can't unsee it!. The dish is a tricky assembly to get right, orientation wise with the kit parts & how they sit to the sidewall cut-outs.

Reactions : Like, Love — Bjorn, jcoffman99, Necronaut
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

John,

Nice clean work. Great job done on collecting the very small greeblies.

Plan a good insertion method that permits some tolerance to float these (8) completed units in all three directions when inside the mandible skin. Adjustment can be done when placing armor over the original skin holes measurements. In any case, piping geometry is rather fixed on armor surface. Those end points must connect to very specific kit part locations in the maintenance pits. Height of every pit wall has a key greeblie that defines that dimension. But, the pit wall inside diameter can be demanding when terminating piping when the puzzle is glued down in advance.

Allowance for elevation, rotation and horizontal X&Y coordinates was my lesson learned on this design. Constantly amazed on the care given by ILM folks when selecting and matching parts that look realistic and never see the Sun shine.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2459  (forum post #5530497)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Apr 20, 2024 at 5:46 PM
Reactions : Like, Love — JimmyB, Bjorn, Rick Deckard

Post #2513  (forum post #5539057)
Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : May 22, 2024 at 2:10 PM
Reactions : Wow — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

So I’m looking at my dish. I think I’m going with a depth of 3.37mm and creating a lip that is 1.4mm. 

However….

Looking at my part I noticed that the width of the current lip  is not even close to consistent. In some places it measure 3mm. Others, it’s way above or below. From the 12:00 to 3:00 is it considerably wider. 

Are the acrylic ones more uniformed?

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/img_0577-jpeg.1822872/]

I'm still poking around with the lower port mechanical bay and there's still one part I'm not too sure about.

Below is a scaled up image of the Bandai kit part and I'm trying to source the part I've marked as A. It has a diameter of just shy of 20mm and appears to be just a solid disk. Now I know Bandai doesn't get everything perfect but given how much detail they put in places that are hidden, I'd think if this had more details, they would have added it.

So my prospective options are B and C from the Bandai 1/24 Panther G. B is ~almost exactly~ the correct diameter and C is slightly oversized but has a couple of small details on it.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1717545301433-png.1827091/]

If you look at the image below, that part looks to have a notch in the front and a hole through it. But I can't tell if those are shadows, or if they're on the part. Also it looks like there might be something under it too. I don't have any other clear image of this area of the model. I'm probably going to go with B above, unless someone has a more definitive answer to what part that is.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1717545636204-png.1827093/]

Post #2538  (forum post #5544197)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Jun 9, 2024 at 7:57 AM
Reactions : Like — swgeek, Treadwell
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

In the JagdPanther pit, there's an I beam shape that runs along the forward/starboard edge just under the plates. I've not seen it listed on any parts map, but it looks to be a cut down part #14 from the American LaFrance Ladder Chief. Dimensionally it's spot on when you scale up the Bandai PG part, and if you cut it in the right place, you get the upward supports lined up the same way too.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1717943502266-png.1828568/]

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1717943588873-png.1828569/]

Nice way to ask for a little help. Suggest this be done as a PM. No doubt a 5ft. Studio Scale Falcon is nearly impossible to accomplish without the many helpful “guides” on this web site that have taken the journey before us. Even with the relative recent advent of CAD & 3D printing, the dedication & knowledge required to complete reasonable fidelity is staggering. The small pieces do drive me nuts. I am poor at simple casting techniques. So, I mass produce by scratch build place keepers if tiny kit parts are not available when needed.

I'm not sure if this has been answered or not so apologies if it has but the green part on top appears to be from the (most likely) Tamiya 1/35 20mm Flakvierling 38 which wasn't released until 1977 so that puts the timeline after Star Wars and about right for Empire.

To celebrate scifikitbash.com's second birthday today, I have added a section for trading parts. I hope this will be a place where fellow members can find those missing parts, or casts of parts they need to finish their builds. This is for trading only. THERE IS NO SELLING OF PARTS ALLOWED. When money comes into play, my job gets tougher. 

There is also a subforum for member feedback.

We'll see how this goes. If it works great! If not, I'll pull it down.

Thanks,

John

Post #2658  (forum post #5584469)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Nov 10, 2024 at 6:30 AM
Reactions : Like — manugu, joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I've gone through the motorized amour and ship kits that I have access to used for this build. All the pinion gears are ~7mm, we are looking for 11mm. (determined by scaling up the Bandai PG kit gear)

If it is a real gear (it sort of looks it), it could be just a found part from anything they had laying around, and not from a kit at all.

For kicks, I popped open a couple of RC servos to have a look. Nothing useful.

[edit] This morning I rummaged through the racing car kits, the kettlegrad, the BMW motorcycle, the Tamiya racing bike, the big M60, and a few additional smaller kits. No joy.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Studio Kitbash said:

			Nice job!  That looks WAY better than my first mold's version.
		

Click to expand...

I've found that for small parts like these it's best NOT to use a pressure pot. I open the mold as far as it will go and shoot the resin into the tiny bits like the circles. Then I close it up and shoot in the rest. Seems to be working. The issues with this one are actually in the mold itself which I'm trying to cut/clean up. Hopefully the next one will be perfect.

In the case of the first version, the C6 and C7 cannot be used for the 5-foot Falcon.

"1750" means "1750 yen". "12009-700" is an unnatural number. Is the right edge covered with a label or something? This kit should be "7000 yen".

Post #2711  (forum post #5593642)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Dec 21, 2024 at 3:48 PM
Reactions : Like, Wow — joberg, TDahl, manugu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Small correction on the part maps. 'Starboard Sidewall Rear' (and anywhere else this casting is used) calls for using the jack from #61 Hummel.

The top one is from the Hummel.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1734825136413-png.1890138/]

The lower one is the jack from #62 Assault Gun IV. It has the little holes in it.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Another small correction on the part maps.

The below, I believe, should be part D3 of the #122 Nichimo King Tiger 1/48 scale. The part map lists #123, the Nichimo Elefant.

This observation is largely based off of Stu's images above, and some additional review of references. The Elefant does have a similar part, but it can not possibly fit.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1735251598482-png.1891247/]

thanks

Post #2735  (forum post #5595920)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Jan 1, 2025 at 5:10 PM
Reactions : Sad — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Hi.

Nope, I've looked at all the gears in the motorized/RC kits (at least the ones I have in my stable). They're too small. The one we are looking for has an outside diameter of ~11mm and 12 teeth.

If you disassemble the transmissions from the Assault Gun or the Hummel, there's a gear in there that's close, but it's still not correct overall. There's a gear in the 1/25 Centurion that is close to the right diameter, but it only has 10 teeth.

The other thing with the part used on the filming model is that it has a bit of a rounded crown to it. I'm not sure why a gear would have that. I'm not sure why a heat sink would have that. That rounded top is really the only thing that makes me think it's a kit part, but I'm not sure.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I asked this before in another thread. Never got a definitive answer, there may not be one, ; ) Does anyone know if the maintenance pits in the mandibles are directly over one another. Or are they offset. Just the sort of nitpicky thing that triggers my OCD. There's no way to see the top and bottom at the same time, without a mirror, but that's my jam, lol.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2766  (forum post #5613606)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Mar 18, 2025 at 2:36 AM
Reactions : Like — bwayne64, joberg

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2767  (forum post #5613620)
Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Mar 18, 2025 at 6:24 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, manugu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

bwayne64, your OCD serves you well...

They may or may not be "directly" over each other, but they are NOT in identical locations top to bottom or left to right.  They are all slightly offset, and you learn this the hard way if you cut your circles first, instead of deriving your circle placement from first doing greeblies on sidewalls, and then doing armour-coating notched-Plastruct sheeting on top as you work your way "in" to the circle location.  If it looks right, then the exact dimensional location is less significant than the parts-to-the-whole relationship of maintenance pit to the rest of the mandible surface.

So I would say, all said, that they are "roughly" over each other, but not "directly."  That level of geometric perfection was only achieved/faked by the Bandai kit versions of this thing, which they achieved only through CAD design and tweaking.  THAT said, they are "over each other" enough that you can't make any one maintenance pit so deep that it exceeds the opposite side's maintenance pit depth, and that fact alone "may or may not" be a factor in why some pit depths are so significantly different from other pit depths -- in other words, the pit depth may "not" have been derived from max greeblie height, but instead derived from opposite side pit depth, and secondarily greeblies were found/modified to fit a new/lower pit depth on the opposite side.  I can't say defnitively this is what happened, but it is a distinct possibility.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2768  (forum post #5613669)
Author    : Searun
Date      : Mar 18, 2025 at 9:11 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, bwayne64, Necronaut
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

On my build the side wall height was the most critical. Thanks to those before me who advised that number. The pontoon bridge fit perfectly into the gap set between the top and lower skin. 

This is an inside dimension at the wall edge as the disc top and bottom airfoils slope to it. Unless pit floor plate construction is too thick or wall depth is too great, the math works without interference. My pits readily slid in place back and forth to match the location shown by the 3” mandible holes thanks to Andre’s excellent drawings. 

Note the geometry of the many bottom and top piping chase runs to and from the pits help confirm pit circle location in the mandible surface. None of my pits seemed to be directly over each other as mentioned by SK above.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #2785  (forum post #5615959)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Mar 27, 2025 at 4:45 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, Shamon, Dazza007
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I can't speak for the diameter, but the part on the left has too many teeth.

I found this. An 11mm, 12 tooth, spur gear from a 1970s era FUTABA radio control sail boat servo. Almost certainly not the correct part, but dimensionally it's correct and has the correct number of teeth. Fill it with a bit of 5 minute epoxy (which is what ILM used) and you'd have that crowned top as well.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1743119016276-png.1917862/]

No Tamiya 1/25 Chieftains are required for the Falcon. The 1/35 is needed though.

It could be a composite part. I don't know if they cast these sub-assemblies, but the open end of the Tamiya Panzer III part wouldn't likely cast very well and might have been covered with something else to allow casting. The end looks like a separate part that was cut to fit.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2834  (forum post #5629528)
Author    : manugu
Date      : May 16, 2025 at 3:48 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

There seems to have been a change in numbering, kits had a "pattern number" up to ~1959, then a "catalogue number" and then a "computerized cataloge number" introduced in the early 1970es. On top of that, there are different packaging variants that should contain the same kit - as Treadwell already mentioned.

References:

(link at the bottom of this webpage - Additional Resources: History of Airfix)

			https://d63oxfkn1m8sf.cloudfront.net/1516/4812/2570/Kilt_List_6_-_Digital_Edition.pdf
		

			Airfix Collectors Club
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2835  (forum post #5629559)
Author    : Shamon
Date      : May 16, 2025 at 6:20 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg, manugu
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

manugu said:

			There seems to have been a change in numbering, kits had a "pattern number" up to ~1959, then a "catalogue number" and then a "cumputerized cataloge number" introduced in the early 1970es. On top of that, there are different packaging variants that should contain the same kit - as Treadwell already mentioned.

References:

(link at the bottom of this webpage - Additional Resources: History of Airfix)

			https://d63oxfkn1m8sf.cloudfront.net/1516/4812/2570/Kilt_List_6_-_Digital_Edition.pdf
		

			Airfix Collectors Club
		

Click to expand...

Thank you, manugu. It means that there was a time when the old number and the new number were displayed together when the numbering method was changed. I was relieved to know the reason.

Thanks to Studio Kitbash, I found out that the gear that was unidentified was a part of the German Tank IV-F2. Knowing this result, one thing came up with me that I was curious about. Who was the first person to say that this part is Hummel? As you know, many parts are shared in the three kits of Hummel, Tank IV-F2, and Assault Gun4. This is an imagination, but maybe there was an vintage Hummel kit that was not posted on Scale mates, and this gear was included in it.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

AI is crazy. This is the summary that Gemini created for the kit list document on my drive:

This document is a guide for building a 5-foot Millennium Falcon model using various donor kits. It lists kits from manufacturers like Airfix, AMT, Bandai, and others, with information such as kit numbers, scales, and prices. The guide is updated as the builder progresses and aims to be a reference for future builders. It includes a table of contents and detailed lists of specific kits organized by manufacturer.

Skynet be crazy yo.

Can I propose "Shamon" as a new word for the hobby?  

I feel deep Shamon at this latest post.  To my mind, the word Shamon means this:

1.) The feeling of shame that you did not see, identify, or know this piece.

2.) The equal feeling of gratitude that someone, namely Shamon, DID come along to tell you.

Shamon:  the feeling of shame and gratitude in equal measure for a new ID that was "hiding in plain sight" all along...

Deep Shamon:  the feeling of shame and gratitude in equal measure for a new ID, with the added sensation that this isn't the last time this is going to happen...

I'm not sayin' I was a Falcon expert before Shamon came along.  I'm just sayin' that Shamon makes me feel like a rookie every time he posts... 

Thanks Shamon!  You are legend.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Post #2874  (forum post #5638466)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Jun 20, 2025 at 2:14 PM
Reactions : Like — joberg, jcoffman99
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

One of the keys to note is that the big Kettenkrad part isn't perfectly centered. It's offset slightly.

I discovered I cut down that part too much and so had to re-add a small bit of styrene at the bottom to make up the difference. You'll never see it once painted, but just something to be aware of, don't cut it down as far as I did.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/img_4828-jpg.1943014/]

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/img_4829-jpg.1943015/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I use that technique all the time. It looks like you have a better camera than I do though as you're getting sharper details.

The thing is to double and triple check the plate pattern that Bandai uses. They get it wrong in a lot of places. The plating on the lower engineering deck for example is something they completely made up.

Post #2884  (forum post #5644393)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Jul 14, 2025 at 7:25 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The plating differences between the model kit and the filming model look to be driven mostly by the fact that Bandai did everything in CAD and they made things symmetrical. If you compare the lower rear engine/landing gear deck from the kit (that I pointed to in the video), to the filming model, you can see very distinct differences in the location of panel lines and the little notches. There's a non-symmetrical 'wonky-ness' to the real model that the kit ignores.

Also some of the kit's molded in greeblies are oversized (when scaled up), which pushes panel lines in further out of whack. So it's just something to keep an eye out for.

That gun platform doesn't scale up to the correct size. The diameter of that part is ~3.35", which scaled up is ~11.75" but the diameter on published drawings is 11.50" -  assuming I've done my measurements and math correctly, and that the published drawings are correct. Which is possible because there are errors on those drawings, at least the ones I have.

It is a good starting point though. Like I said, I did the same thing, and it will get you very close.
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
