I think it might be part #2 from the AMT 1/25 K-123 Cabover, specifically the inside cab rear "sleeper walls" piece.  I feel certain this came up in somebody else's thread, but can't seem to find it, but here are pictures of that piece, which looks like it's been cut in about half (at 27 ribs) and maybe trimmed vertically as well.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1757873108346-jpeg.1970262/]

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1757873135918-jpeg.1970263/]

Here it is in situ on the sprue.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1757873162190-jpeg.1970264/]

Here it is off the sprue -- seems to have the same raised/rounded aspect to each rib that the V-Groove sheet pattern does not have.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1757873273240-jpeg.1970265/]

Here it is against a ruler and cutting mat, in case you want to count and compare the number of grooves per inch vs. the real prop.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1757873353203-jpeg.1970266/]

Here's the width of it:  23.02mm in original form.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/1757873423605-jpeg.1970267/]

Here's the length of it in orginal form:  93.19mm

If anyone can confirm or deny, I'd appreciate it.  And if it's categorically "NOT" the piece, then I still think it looks better and works better as a substitute than V-groove Evergreen sheet, which is totally flat and lifeless and, um... boring?  Because to my mind, anything that's not an actual greeblie from a donor kit is, by definition, boring.  So in my build philosophy I'd rather have the wrong greeblie than a right-looking piece of stock styrene.

Studio Kitbash said:

			I think it might be part #2 from the AMT 1/25 K-123 Cabover, specifically the inside cab rear "sleeper walls" piece.  I feel certain this came up in somebody else's thread, but can't seem to find it, but here are pictures of that piece, which looks like it's been cut in about half (at 27 ribs) and maybe trimmed vertically as well.

View attachment 1970262

View attachment 1970263

Here it is in situ on the sprue.

View attachment 1970264

Here it is off the sprue -- seems to have the same raised/rounded aspect to each rib that the V-Groove sheet pattern does not have.

View attachment 1970265

Here it is against a ruler and cutting mat, in case you want to count and compare the number of grooves per inch vs. the real prop.

View attachment 1970266

Here's the width of it:  23.02mm in original form.

View attachment 1970267

Here's the length of it in orginal form:  93.19mm

If anyone can confirm or deny, I'd appreciate it.  And if it's categorically "NOT" the piece, then I still think it looks better and works better as a substitute than V-groove Evergreen sheet, which is totally flat and lifeless and, um... boring?  Because to my mind, anything that's not an actual greeblie from a donor kit is, by definition, boring.  So in my build philosophy I'd rather have the wrong greeblie than a right-looking piece of stock styrene.
		

Click to expand...

Really like your philosophy on “boring” as the default. Must say that it appears as if the Evergreen sheet is a shoe that fits and even satisfies the some Jedi & Guru folks.

When you guys are making your sidewall masters to mold, what thickness styrene are you mounting them to? 2mm? 3? 800???

Thanks,

John
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2913  (forum post #5662620)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Sep 15, 2025 at 8:42 AM
Reactions : Like — Necronaut, Shamon, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I used 1mm. If they need to extend further off the core, I can add additional sheets behind. They also need to be thin enough to wrap around the curve.

jcoffman99 said:

My master pattern sidewalls were laid up on a base plate of 2mm. You need this thickness as a minimum, to give the wall casts depth.

That last picture is priceless -- that ****-eating grin of you inside the cockpit tube reminds me of the same smile I had on my face when I got the largest model kit in the entire universe for my 1978 birthday, and inside was a Millennium Falcon model the size of Texas.  I remember distinctly, as a nine-year old, wondering HOW and WHY the universe (and my Aunt Louise) could produce such marvels.

Also, another funny side bit:  two years ago I had a chance to go visit Galaxy's Edge with my childhood best friend, but he's still so pissed off at Kathleen Kennedy for "raping his childhood" with the new franchise films that he refuses to give a single penny to Disney Corp.  I tried to cajole him off the edge, "Dude, it's the Millennium Falcon!" but he was resolute, and though we were but 20 minutes away at a model convention, he was completely unwilling to do it.  So it's still on my bucket list and until then I remain green with envy.

Studio Scale Modeler's Relationship Advice:  Top Ten List of Sentences to Never Say Out Loud in the Presence of Your Wife:

1.)  "There is no greater feeling than pulling the hyperdrive lever on the Smuggler's Run ride."

2.)  Actually, that outfit does make you look a little bit chubby.

3.)  No, darling, I'm not fantasizing a week later about Galaxy's Edge; I was actually thinking about you.

4.)  Hey, waddya say?  Do you think we should go to Disney World every anniversary from now on?

5.)  "Sweetheart, don't be so angry because of a misunderstanding; what I said was, 'Snuggler's Run'!!!"

6.)  Disneyvorce?  NO, I said Disney Vacation!  Sweetie, Sweetie, get back in the car...

7.)

8.)

9.)

10.)  Actually, I've been told mine is a little small...

Please, help me fill these in...

Author    : jcoffman99
Date      : Jan 17, 2026 at 5:32 AM
Reactions : Like — joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Could use some advice here from those that have molded this part. Did you do a two piece or were you able to do it in one. I’m perplexed on how to handle the part that my fingers are on. 

Thanks!

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/img_7700-jpeg.2004866/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Post #2929  (forum post #5694775)
Author    : RealDaveWinter
Date      : Jan 17, 2026 at 5:53 AM
Reactions : Like — manugu, joberg, Studio Kitbash
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

I created a two part mold

I push a syringe into holes you see on the right half and inject the resin, sort of like injection molding plastic. Then I put the whole thing into a pressure pot. I use SmoothCast 310, it takes about 4 hours to cure, but I get zero bubbles.

For that exact portion of the part your fingers are on, I didn't cast that bit, but if I remember correctly, it has a perfectly flat back. So you could get away with a single part mold for that bit.

Hope that helps.

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/img_5251-jpg.2004868/]
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Author    : Studio Kitbash
Date      : Jan 17, 2026 at 6:38 AM

You can do a one-piece if you cast it vertically instead of flat, but you do get better results with a two-piece, I've got both.

Reactions : Like, Wow — thd9791, Searun, joberg
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Not purely Falcon related:

A while ago some of us started to build a collection of the model kits (most likely) present in the ILM Van Nuys model shop during creation of the original Star Wars. For this we IDed kits in old photos of the ILM shelves an correlated that with the known donor kits for each model.

Small teaser of the most famous image:

[IMAGE: https://www.therpf.com/forums/attachments/ilm_shop-jpg.2010789/]

We did this for ~30 more 
[IMAGE: yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7]
 

More on Scifikitbash:

			SciFiKitBash
